Each block has a specific, non-negotiable role:

- **Transducer/Electrode** — converts ionic current (in body) into electronic current (in circuit). Examples: Ag/AgCl electrode, piezoelectric crystal, photodiode.
- **Signal Conditioner** — combines an **instrumentation amplifier** (gain 100 – 10,000), a **band-pass filter** (e.g., 0.05 – 150 Hz for ECG), and **isolation** (patient safety).
- **Display/Recorder** — CRO, digital oscilloscope, LCD, or computer.

### 2.3 Transducers — Active vs Passive

| Type | Working Principle | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Active Transducer** | Self-generates voltage (no external power) | Piezoelectric crystal, thermocouple, EMG electrode |
| **Passive Transducer** | Requires external excitation (changes R, L, C) | Strain gauge, LVDT, thermistor, photoresistor |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — these are the high-yield formulas for KTU 2024 ESE questions.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = G \cdot V_{in}$ | Output of an amplifier with gain $G$ |
| 2 | $G_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | Gain in decibels (voltage ratio) |
| 3 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{s}}{V_{n}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio in dB |
| 4 | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Cut-off frequency of first-order RC filter |
| 5 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio (dB) |
| 6 | $Z_{input} = \dfrac{V}{I} \;\big\vert\;_{V \to 0}$ | Input impedance definition (must be high for bio-signals) |
| 7 | $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta X_{in}}$ | Transducer sensitivity |
| 8 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency (rad/s) |
| 9 | $V_{RMS} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS value of a sine wave |
| 10 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Range}}{2^{n}}$ | ADC resolution with $n$ bits |
| 11 | $q = 0.693 \cdot \text{C}$ | Charge in coulombs for half-cell potential |
| 12 | $V_{cell} = V_{Ag} - V_{Cl} \approx +0.222\text{ V}$ | Ag/AgCl electrode half-cell potential (vs SHE) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Prosthetic Limbs** use EMG waveforms to decode the user's intent and actuate motors.
- **Pacemakers** use ECG waveforms to detect arrhythmias and deliver corrective pulses.
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** use EEG to control external devices (wheelchairs, cursors).
- **Sleep Labs** use EEG/EOG/EMG polysomnography to diagnose disorders like sleep apnea.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 3: Waveforms and Measurements — Section 3

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cut-off Frequency of a First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter

The most common signal conditioning element in any biomedical instrument is the **RC low-pass filter** (used to remove high-frequency noise from ECG, EMG, etc.).

**Step 1 — Define the circuit.**
A resistor $R$ is in series, and a capacitor $C$ is connected from the output node to ground. Input voltage is $V_{in}(t)$ and output is $V_{out}(t)$ across the capacitor.

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider rule in the frequency domain (using impedances).**

$$Z_{R} = R \qquad\text{and}\qquad Z_{C} = \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage transfer function (output/input ratio).**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{Z_{C}}{Z_{R} + Z_{C}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the impedance values.**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$ to simplify.**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 6 — Take the magnitude of the transfer function.**

$$\left\vert \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \right\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^{2}}}$$

**Step 7 — Define the cut-off frequency** as the frequency at which the output magnitude drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ (or $-3$ dB) of the input. At this point, $\omega RC = 1$.

$$\omega_{c} \cdot RC = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega_{c} = \frac{1}{RC}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to Hertz using $\omega = 2\pi f$.**

$$\boxed{\,f_{c} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}\,}$$

**Step 9 — Interpret the result.**
- For ECG: use $f_{c} \approx 150$ Hz (filters out high-frequency muscle noise above cardiac range).
- For EEG: use $f_{c} \approx 30$ Hz (filters out EMG and 50/60 Hz power-line interference).
- For EMG: use $f_{c} \approx 5$ kHz (preserves sharp motor unit spikes).

### 3.2 Derivation: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) Improvement

**Step 1 — Define the original SNR.**

$$\text{SNR}_{in} = \frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the original SNR in dB.**

$$\text{SNR}_{in,\,dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply an amplifier with voltage gain $G$.**
Both signal and noise are amplified equally, so:

$$\text{SNR}_{out} = \frac{G \cdot V_{signal}}{G \cdot V_{noise}} = \frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
An **ideal amplifier does NOT improve SNR**. The SNR can only be improved by **filtering** (removing noise frequencies outside the signal band) or by using **low-noise components** (e.g., JFET input stages).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Frequency Band vs Required Filter

| Signal | Bandwidth (Hz) | High-Pass $f_{c}$ | Low-Pass $f_{c}$ | Typical Amplitude |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ECG** | **0.05 – 150** | 0.05 Hz | 150 Hz | 0.5 – 5 mV |
| **EMG** | **20 – 5000** | 20 Hz | 5000 Hz | 50 µV – 5 mV |
| **EEG** | **0.5 – 30** | 0.5 Hz | 30 Hz | 1 – 100 µV |
| **EOG** | **DC – 30** | DC (0 Hz) | 30 Hz | 0.05 – 3.5 mV |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A first-order RC low-pass filter is to be designed for an EMG signal conditioner. The cut-off frequency required is 5 kHz. If the resistor value is $R = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, calculate the required capacitor $C$.

**Step 1 — Write the cut-off frequency formula.**

$$f_{c} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $C$.**

$$C = \frac{1}{2\pi R f_{c}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given values.**

$$C = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (1 \times 10^{3}) \times (5 \times 10^{3})}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$2\pi \times 1 \times 10^{3} \times 5 \times 10^{3} = 10\pi \times 10^{6} \approx 3.1416 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation.**

$$\boxed{\,C \approx 3.18 \times 10^{-8}\text{ F} = 31.8\text{ nF}\,}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 3: Waveforms and Measurements — Section 4

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Biomedical Measurement System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceA[BIOLOGICAL SOURCE\nHeart, Brain, Muscle, Eye] --> transB[TRANSDUCER\nOR ELECTRODE\nAg/AgCl, Piezoelectric]
    transB --> condC[SIGNAL CONDITIONER\nAmplifier, Filter, Isolation]
    condC --> dispD[DISPLAY OR RECORDER\nCRO, LCD, Computer, Printer]
    condD1[ACTUATOR OR FEEDBACK\nProsthetic Motor, Pacemaker, Alarm] -.-> dispD
    dispD --> actE[CLINICIAN OR\nCONTROL SYSTEM]
    actE -->|Decision| condC
```

### 4.2 ECG Waveform PQRST Complex — Labeled Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA([START OF ECG COMPLEX]) --> pwave[P WAVE\nAtrial Depolarization\n0.25 mV, 0.10 s]
    pwave --> prseg[PR SEGMENT\nIsoelectric line\nAV node delay]
    prseg --> print[PR INTERVAL\n0.12 to 0.20 s]
    print --> qrs[QRS COMPLEX\nVentricular Depolarization\n1.00 mV, 0.08 s]
    qrs --> stseg[ST SEGMENT\nIsoelectric\n0.05 to 0.15 s]
    stseg --> twave[T WAVE\nVentricular Repolarization\n0.50 mV, 0.16 s]
    twave --> uwave[U WAVE\nPurkinje Repolarization\n0.10 mV optional]
    uwave --> qtint[QT INTERVAL\n0.36 to 0.44 s]
    qtint --> endA([END OF COMPLEX])
```

### 4.3 Bio-Signal Frequency Spectrum Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    freqA[FREQUENCY 0 Hz] --> freqB[DELTA 0.5 to 4 Hz]
    freqB --> freqC[THETA 4 to 8 Hz]
    freqC --> freqD[ALPHA 8 to 13 Hz]
    freqD --> freqE[BETA 13 to 30 Hz]
    freqE --> freqF[GAMMA greater than 30 Hz]
    freqF --> freqG[EMG up to 5 kHz]
```

### 4.4 Transducer Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classA[TRANSDUCERS] --> actB[ACTIVE TRANSDUCER\nSelf-generating voltage]
    classA --> pasC[PASSIVE TRANSDUCER\nNeeds external excitation]
    actB --> actD[Piezoelectric\nThermocouple\nPhotovoltaic]
    pasC --> pasE[Strain Gauge\nLVDT\nThermistor\nLDR]
```

### 4.5 Signal Conditioning Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawA[RAW BIO SIGNAL\nmicrovolt to millivolt] --> instB[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER\nGain 100 to 10000\nHigh CMRR greater than 80 dB]
    instB --> hpC[HIGH PASS FILTER\nRemoves DC and baseline drift]
    hpC --> lpD[LOW PASS FILTER\nRemoves high frequency noise]
    lpD --> isoE[ISOLATION AMPLIFIER\nPatient safety 5 kV isolation]
    isoE --> outF[CLEAN PROCESSED SIGNAL\nReady for ADC and display]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 3: Waveforms and Measurements — Section 5

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**List the five main components of a generalized biomedical measurement system. State the function of each in one line.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Biological Source / Patient** | Origin of the physiological signal (heart, brain, muscle) |
| 2 | **Transducer / Electrode** | Converts physiological (ionic) energy into electrical energy |
| 3 | **Signal Conditioner** | Amplifies, filters, and isolates the weak bioelectric signal |
| 4 | **Display / Recorder** | Visualizes the signal (CRO, LCD, computer) for clinical use |
| 5 | **Actuator / Feedback** | Produces therapeutic or assistive response (motor, alarm) |

*Mark Distribution: 1 mark for naming the block, 1 mark for stating function, 1 mark for biomedical example.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**
**Define the term "bioelectric waveform." Give two examples with their typical amplitude and frequency range.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A bioelectric waveform is the time-varying graphical record of the electrical potential difference generated by excitable biological tissues (cells, organs), captured using electrodes and a suitable recording system.

> **Example 1 — ECG (1 mark):** Amplitude $\approx$ **0.5 – 5 mV**; Frequency range $\approx$ **0.05 – 150 Hz**; represents cardiac electrical activity (PQRST complex).

> **Example 2 — EEG (1 mark):** Amplitude $\approx$ **1 – 100 µV**; Frequency range $\approx$ **0.5 – 30 Hz**; represents cortical brain activity (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma rhythms).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the generalized biomedical measurement system. List the static and dynamic characteristics of a measurement system. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **Block Diagram (3 Marks):** Already covered in Section 4.1 above. The student must draw 5 blocks: Biological Source → Transducer → Signal Conditioner → Display/Recorder → Actuator/Feedback, and label the arrows.

> **Static Characteristics (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Accuracy** — closeness of measured value to true value
> 2. **Precision** — reproducibility of measurement
> 3. **Sensitivity** — output per unit input
> 4. **Resolution** — smallest detectable change
> 5. **Linearity** — constant sensitivity across the operating range
> 6. **Drift** — change of output with time/temperature
> 7. **Threshold** — minimum input to produce a detectable output

> **Dynamic Characteristics (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Speed of Response / Rise Time** — time to reach 90% of final value
> 2. **Time Constant** — $\tau = RC$ for first-order systems
> 3. **Settling Time** — time to settle within $\pm 2$% of final value
> 4. **Bandwidth / Frequency Response** — range of frequencies over which the system responds linearly
> 5. **Dynamic Error** — difference between true and measured value under varying input
> 6. **Lag** — delay between input change and output response

**(b) Explain the ECG waveform in detail. Sketch the standard PQRST complex, label the waves, intervals, and segments, and state the physiological origin of each wave. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **Sketch (3 Marks):** Use the diagram from Section 4.2. The student must draw amplitude (mV) on the Y-axis and time (s) on the X-axis, and clearly mark P, Q, R, S, T, and U waves. PR interval, QRS duration, ST segment, and QT interval must be labeled.

> **Wave-by-Wave Description (4 Marks):**

| Wave | Physiological Origin | Amplitude | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization (SA node fires) | 0.25 mV | 0.08 – 0.10 s |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization (Bundle of His → Purkinje fibers) | 1.00 mV | 0.06 – 0.10 s |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 0.50 mV | 0.16 s |
| **U wave** | Purkinje fiber repolarization (sometimes absent) | 0.10 mV | 0.10 s |
| **PR interval** | Conduction time from atria to ventricles (includes AV nodal delay of ~0.10 s) | — | 0.12 – 0.20 s |
| **QT interval** | Total ventricular electrical activity (depolarization + repolarization) | — | 0.36 – 0.44 s |
| **ST segment** | All ventricular cells are depolarized (isoelectric) | 0 mV | 0.05 – 0.15 s |

> **Clinical note (bonus, 1 mark):** Elevation of ST segment indicates myocardial infarction; prolonged QT interval indicates risk of arrhythmia.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a) Compare active and passive transducers. Give two biomedical examples of each. With the help of a circuit, derive the cut-off frequency of a first-order RC low-pass filter and explain its significance in ECG signal conditioning. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Active Transducer | Passive Transducer |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | Self-generating | Needs external excitation |
| Output | Voltage/current directly | Change in R, L, C |
| Examples | Piezoelectric, Thermocouple, Solar cell, EMG electrode | Strain gauge, LVDT, Thermistor, LDR, RTD |

> **Biomedical Examples (1 Mark):**
> - Active: Piezoelectric crystal in ultrasound probe, Ag/AgCl biopotential electrode
> - Passive: Strain gauge in a force plate, LVDT in a prosthetic joint position sensor

> **Derivation of Cut-off Frequency (3 Marks):**
> (Full derivation already given in Section 3.1 of these notes.) Final result:

$$f_{c} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

> **Significance in ECG Signal Conditioning (1 Mark):**
> The ECG signal has useful information in the band **0.05 – 150 Hz**. The low-pass filter is used to cut off frequencies above 150 Hz, removing high-frequency muscle noise (EMG contamination) and electromagnetic interference. With $R = 1$ k$\Omega$ and $C = 1$ µF, we get $f_{c} \approx 159$ Hz, which is just above the ECG upper limit — a perfect fit.

**(b) Explain the origin, characteristics, and clinical applications of EEG signals. Draw the frequency spectrum and label all five standard rhythms. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **Origin (2 Marks):** EEG is the recording of the **summed post-synaptic potentials of cortical neurons** (pyramidal cells in the cerebral cortex) using scalp electrodes (10–20 international system). The signal is generated by ionic current flow across neuronal membranes and picked up via Ag/AgCl electrodes.

> **Characteristics (2 Marks):**
> - Amplitude: **1 – 100 µV** (extremely weak — needs amplification $\geq$ 10,000×)
> - Frequency: **0.5 – 30 Hz** (low frequency, requires AC coupling)
> - High source impedance ($\sim$ 10 k$\Omega$) — needs high-input-impedance amplifier
> - Susceptible to 50/60 Hz mains interference — requires notch filter

> **Five Standard Rhythms — Frequency Spectrum (2 Marks):**

| Rhythm | Frequency Band | State |
|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep sleep, infants, pathological in awake adults |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness, meditation, light sleep |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | Active thinking, alert, anxious |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz | Cognitive processing, perception, memory |

> **Clinical Applications (1 Mark):**
> 1. **Epilepsy diagnosis** — abnormal spike-and-wave discharges
> 2. **Sleep studies (polysomnography)** — identification of sleep stages
> 3. **Brain death confirmation** — flat-line EEG
> 4. **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — controlling external devices using alpha/beta rhythms
> 5. **Anesthesia depth monitoring** — BIS (Bispectral Index)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the units:** ECG is in **mV**, EEG is in **µV** — mixing these up is a 1-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 2. **Missing the block arrows in the measurement system diagram** — arrows are mandatory; a box without an arrow is incomplete.
> 3. **Forgetting clinical significance** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes outcome-based learning; a pure theory answer without clinical/engineering relevance loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong formula in the derivation** — using $f_{c} = 1/RC$ instead of $1/(2\pi RC)$ is a direct 1-mark cut.
> 5. **Not stating the electrode material** — Ag/AgCl is the standard biomedical electrode; saying "metal electrode" is too vague.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- **ECG:** PQRST complex, P-wave (atria), QRS (ventricles depolarize), T-wave (ventricles repolarize), PR interval (0.12–0.20 s), QT interval (0.36–0.44 s), QRS duration (0.06–0.10 s), amplitude range **0.5 – 5 mV**, band **0.05 – 150 Hz**.

- **EEG:** 5 rhythms — **Delta 0.5–4 Hz, Theta 4–8 Hz, Alpha 8–13 Hz, Beta 13–30 Hz, Gamma > 30 Hz**, amplitude **1–100 µV**, band **0.5–30 Hz**, recorded with **10–20 electrode placement system**.

- **EMG:** Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP), amplitude **50 µV – 5 mV**, band **20 Hz – 5 kHz**, **surface** and **needle** electrodes.

- **EOG:** Corneo-retinal potential **0.4 – 1.0 mV**, amplitude **0.05 – 3.5 mV**, used for eye-movement tracking and sleep staging.

- **5 Blocks of Biomedical Measurement System:** Source → Transducer → Signal Conditioner → Display/Recorder → Actuator/Feedback.

- **Static vs Dynamic Characteristics:** Static = accuracy, precision, sensitivity, resolution, linearity, drift, threshold. Dynamic = rise time, time constant, settling time, bandwidth, lag, dynamic error.

- **Active vs Passive Transducer:** Active = self-generating (piezoelectric, thermocouple). Passive = needs external excitation (strain gauge, LVDT, thermistor).

- **Standard Electrode:** Ag/AgCl half-cell potential = **+0.222 V** vs Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE). Non-polarizable, low offset, ideal for biopotential recording.

- **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Three-op-amp topology, **CMRR > 80 dB**, gain set by a single external resistor, high input impedance, used universally as the first stage of every bioelectric amplifier.

- **Filter Formulas (must memorize):**
  $$f_{c} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \quad \text{(first-order RC filter)}$$
  $$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$$
  $$\text{SNR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{s}}{V_{n}}\right)$$
  $$\text{Gain (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$$

- **ADC Resolution:** $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{2^{n}}$ where $n$ is the number of bits. For a 12-bit ADC with 5 V range: $\text{Resolution} = 5/4096 \approx 1.22$ mV.

- **Why amplifiers don't improve SNR:** Both signal and noise are amplified equally by the same gain $G$, so the ratio remains unchanged. SNR improvement comes from **filtering**, not amplification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evoked Potentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT864 - BIOMECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Evoked Potentials # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Evoked Potentials (EPs)** are time-locked, stimulus-triggered electrical responses generated by the nervous system in reaction to a specific sensory, cognitive, or motor stimulus, recorded non-invasively from the scalp (surface electrodes), spinal cord, or peripheral nerves. They represent the **summated post-synaptic activity** of thousands of neurons firing synchronously in response to an external stimulus, and are typically extracted from the dominant background EEG/EMG noise by **computerized signal averaging** techniques. > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition Statement** > An Evoked Potential is a stereotyped, reproducible, low-amplitude (microvolt range) bioelectric signal that is **time-locked** to a controlled external stimulus, and whose latency, amplitude, and morphology are used to objectively evaluate the functional integrity of specific sensory and motor neural pathways. --- ## 1.2 Sensory Modalities of Evoked Potentials Evoked Potentials are classified strictly according to the **sensory modality stimulated** and the **neural pathway under assessment**: | Modality | Full Name | Stimulus Used | Primary Pathway Tested | Clinical Use | |----------|-----------|---------------|------------------------|--------------| | **VEP** | Visual Evoked Potential | Pattern reversal (checkerboard) or flash | Retina → Optic nerve → Occipital cortex | Optic neuritis, MS, optic neuropathies | | **AEP / BAEP / BERA** | Auditory Evoked Potential / Brainstem Auditory EP | Click / Tone burst | Cochlea → CN VIII → Brainstem → Cortex | Hearing screening, acoustic neuroma, MS | | **SSEP / SEP** | Somatosensory Evoked Potential | Electrical stimulation of median/tibial nerve | Peripheral nerve → Dorsal column → Cortex | Spinal cord injury, MS, intraoperative monitoring | | **MEP** | Motor Evoked Potential | Transcranial magnetic / electrical stimulation | Motor cortex → Spinal cord → Muscle | Motor pathway assessment, surgical monitoring | | **ERP** | Event-Related Potential | Cognitive (oddball) paradigm | Cortical associative areas | Cognitive/neuropsychological assessment | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Clap in a Noisy Stadium" Imagine a massive cricket stadium where 50,000 fans are randomly shouting (this is your background **EEG noise**, ~50–100 µV). Now imagine a single person claps once at a precise moment. - You will **never hear that single clap** in the random noise. Its voltage is too small (~0.1–10 µV). - BUT, if the same person claps **1,000 times in perfect synchrony**, and you **add (average)** those 1,000 recordings together, the random noise cancels out (positive and negative fluctuations), while the clap (always at the same time after each trigger) adds up to become a clear, audible signal. > This is **exactly** how Evoked Potentials work. The stimulus is the "clap," the EEG is the "crowd noise," and the **signal averager** is the device that adds many trials until the small stimulus-locked response emerges from the noise. > [!NOTE] **Key Intuition** > The evoked potential is **NOT visible in a single trial**. It is a *statistical extraction* — a deterministic biological response that is hidden inside stochastic biological noise and is revealed only through synchronized averaging of many identical stimulus events. --- ## 1.4 Historical and Engineering Significance The discovery of Evoked Potentials by **Dawson (1947)** using photographic superimposition, later replaced by **digital averaging (1960s)**, was one of the founding milestones of modern clinical neurophysiology and biomedical engineering. In the mechatronics context, EP recording systems represent a **classic mechatronic loop**: - **Actuator** (stimulator) → **Plant** (neural pathway) → **Sensor** (surface electrodes) → **Signal conditioner** (amplifier, filter) → **Controller** (averager/computer) → **Display** (waveform). --- ## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Clinical Metrics The following values are **standard, exam-worthy** constants in Evoked Potential recording: - **EEG noise amplitude:** ~ **50 µV** - **Evoked potential amplitude:** ~ **0.1 – 20 µV** (often buried in noise) - **Sampling rate required:** ≥ **1 kHz** (for brainstem AEP up to 10 kHz) - **Standard bandpass filter:** **1 – 3000 Hz** (AEP), **1 – 100 Hz** (VEP), **30 – 500 Hz** (intraoperative) - **Typical averaging trials:** **64 – 2000 sweeps** depending on modality - **Stimulus rate:** 0.5 – 30 Hz (slow to avoid adaptation) - **Electrode impedance:** < **5 kΩ** (international 10–20 system) - **Temperature control:** limb temperature maintained > **32 °C** for SSEP (to prevent latency drift) --- ## 1.6 Why Evoked Potentials Matter in Biomechatronics For a KTU 2024 biomechatronics engineer, EPs are critical because: 1. They provide **objective sensory feedback** for patients with communication deficits (used in BCI). 2. They enable **closed-loop neural prosthetics** — e.g., using SSEP feedback during prosthetic limb control. 3. They are used in **intraoperative monitoring (IONM)** to prevent iatrogenic paralysis during spinal surgery. 4. They serve as **biomarkers** for diagnosing demyelinating diseases (Multiple Sclerosis), and for assessing CNS maturation in neonates. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Time-Locked Signal Averaging The cornerstone of EP recording is the **time-locked averaging** process. The recorded signal at any electrode is the linear superposition of: $$x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$$ where: - $s(t)$ is the deterministic, stimulus-locked evoked potential - $n(t)$ is the stochastic background EEG/EMG noise (assumed zero-mean, uncorrelated with $s(t)$) When $N$ identical trials are acquired and averaged point-by-point: $$\bar{x}(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ s(t) + n_i(t) \right] = s(t) + \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)$$ Because $E[n_i(t)] = 0$ (zero mean noise), the noise term shrinks while $s(t)$ is preserved. --- ## 2.2 The Critical Math of Averaging — Why EPs Emerge from Noise The **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** improves as the **square root** of the number of averaged trials — this is the single most important quantitative concept in EP engineering. Assume signal amplitude $A$ and noise RMS amplitude $\sigma$. - **Before averaging (1 trial):** $$SNR_1 = \frac{A}{\sigma}$$ - **After averaging $N$ trials:** Signal adds coherently (× $N$), noise adds in RMS (× $\sqrt{N}$): $$SNR_N = \frac{N \cdot A}{\sqrt{N} \cdot \sigma} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \frac{A}{\sigma} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_1$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **The Square-Root Law of Averaging** > $$\boxed{SNR_N = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_1}$$ > To improve SNR by a factor of 2, you need 4× more trials. To improve SNR by 10, you need 100× more trials. This is why a noisy patient may require **thousands of sweeps**. --- ## 2.3 Latency, Amplitude, and Morphology — The Three Diagnostic Parameters Every evoked potential waveform is described by **three clinical parameters**: 1. **Latency (ms)** — Time from stimulus onset to a specific peak/trough. - Absolute latency: total conduction time. - Interpeak latency: conduction between two generator sites (e.g., I–V interval in BAEP). 2. **Amplitude (µV)** — Voltage difference between a peak and the preceding/following trough of opposite polarity. - Sensitive to axonal loss, demyelination, and synchrony. 3. **Morphology** — Shape of the waveform; reflects synchrony of neural firing. > [!NOTE] **Clinical Interpretation Rule (High Yield)** > - **Increased latency with normal amplitude** → **Demyelination** (MS, GBS) > - **Decreased amplitude with normal latency** → **Axonal loss** (neuropathy, compression) > - **Absent waveform** → **Severe pathway disruption** (transection, tumor) --- ## 2.4 The Three Phases of Any Evoked Potential Waveform Every EP, regardless of modality, displays a stereotyped waveform divided into: | Phase | Latency Range | Origin | Diagnostic Use | |-------|---------------|--------|----------------| | **Far-field potentials** | 0 – 10 ms (short) | Distant generators (brainstem, peripheral nerve) | Sensitive to alignment of electrodes | | **Near-field potentials** | 10 – 50 ms (medium) | Cortical primary receiving areas | Modality-specific (P100, N20, etc.) | | **Endogenous (cognitive)** | > 100 ms | Cortical association areas | Attention, cognition (P300) | --- ## 2.5 Stimulus Parameters — Engineering Control Variables The mechatronics "actuator" is the **stimulator**, and its parameters are tightly controlled: | Modality | Standard Stimulus | Rate | Intensity | Duration | |----------|------------------|------|-----------|----------| | VEP | Checkerboard pattern reversal | 1 – 2 Hz | High contrast | Continuous | | BAEP | Rarefaction click | 10 – 30 Hz | 60 – 70 dB SL above hearing threshold | 100 µs | | SSEP (median) | Square-wave current pulse | 3 – 5 Hz | Just above motor threshold (motor twitch) | 200 µs | | SSEP (tibial) | Square-wave current pulse | 3 – 5 Hz | Motor + sensory threshold | 200 µs | --- ## 2.6 Electrode Montage — The 10–20 International System Surface electrodes follow the **Jasper 10–20 International System**: - **Active (exploring) electrode** placed over the cortical generator. - **Reference electrode** placed on a relatively inactive site (earlobe, Fz, linked ears). - **Ground electrode** placed on forehead or shoulder. **Standard placements:** - VEP → Oz (active), Fz (reference) - BAEP → Cz–A1/A2 (vertex to earlobe, ipsilateral to stimulation) - SSEP (upper limb) → C3'/C4' (contralateral parietal) - SSEP (lower limb) → Cz' (1–2 cm behind Cz) --- ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Sheet for Evoked Potentials** | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units | |---------|---------|------------------|-------| | Signal + Noise Model | $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ | $s$ = EP signal, $n$ = background noise | µV | | Averaging Output | $\bar{x}(t) = s(t) + \frac{1}{N}\sum n_i(t)$ | $N$ = number of trials | — | | **Square-Root SNR Law** | $SNR_N = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_1$ | SNR improvement | Dimensionless | | Required Trials for SNR gain $K$ | $N = K^2$ | To get $K$-fold improvement | Trials | | Conduction Velocity | $CV = \dfrac{D}{\Delta t}$ | $D$ = distance, $\Delta t$ = latency difference | m/s | | Interpeak Latency | $IPL = L_2 - L_1$ | Conduction time between generators | ms | | Amplitude Measurement | $A = \vert V_{peak} - V_{trough} \vert$ | Peak-to-trough voltage | µV | | Central Conduction Time (CCT) | $CCT = L_{cortical} - L_{cervical}$ | Brainstem/cord → cortex | ms | | Amplifier Common Mode Rejection | $CMRR = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Differential/common mode gain | dB | | Noise Reduction by Filtering | $V_{noise,out} = \sqrt{\int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_n(f) \, df}$ | Band-limited noise | µV RMS | | Brainstem I–V Interpeak | $I{-}V \leq 4.6$ ms (adult) | Normal upper limit | ms | | VEP P100 Latency | $P100 \leq 117$ ms (adult) | Normal upper limit | ms | | SSEP N20 Latency (median) | $N20 \leq 22$ ms (adult) | Normal upper limit | ms | > **Strict Notation Note:** All absolute-value operations are written using `\vert ... \vert` to avoid markdown table conflicts. The student must copy these formulas as plain LaTeX. --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility of Evoked Potentials 1. **Intraoperative Neuro-Monitoring (IONM):** SSEP + MEP monitored in real time during spinal deformity surgery to prevent paraplegia. 2. **Neonatal Hearing Screening (BERA):** Universal newborn screening in India under the NPPCD program. 3. **Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis:** VEP P100 delay is the most sensitive objective test for optic neuritis. 4. **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI):** P300 ERP used to spell words for locked-in patients (ALS, brainstem stroke). 5. **Depth of Anesthesia Monitoring:** AEP (MLAEP — middle latency) tracks consciousness. 6. **Prosthetic Sensory Feedback:** SSEPs measured during prosthetic use to verify intact afferent pathways. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Square-Root Improvement Law of Signal Averaging > **Goal:** Prove that averaging $N$ trials improves the SNR by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$. **Assumptions:** - The true EP signal $s(t)$ is deterministic and identical in every trial. - The noise $n_i(t)$ in the $i$-th trial is uncorrelated with $s(t)$ and across trials. - The noise has zero mean, $E[n_i(t)] = 0$, and variance $\sigma^2$. **Step 1 — Recorded single trial:** $$x_i(t) = s(t) + n_i(t)$$ **Step 2 — Define the ensemble average over $N$ trials:** $$\bar{x}_N(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i(t)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the model into the average:** $$\bar{x}_N(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ s(t) + n_i(t) \right] = s(t) + \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)$$ Reason: $s(t)$ is identical in every trial, so $\frac{1}{N}\sum s(t) = s(t)$. Only the noise term gets attenuated. **Step 4 — Compute the expected value of the averaged noise term:** $$E\!\left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t) \right] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} E[n_i(t)] = 0$$ Reason: Each $E[n_i(t)] = 0$, so the expected value of the averaged noise is exactly zero. **Step 5 — Compute the variance of the averaged noise:** $$\text{Var}\!\left( \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t) \right) = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Var}(n_i(t)) = \frac{N\sigma^2}{N^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$$ Reason: The noise samples are independent, so variances add. The factor $1/N^2$ from the averaging operation multiplies the sum of $N$ equal variances $\sigma^2$. **Step 6 — Define SNR after averaging:** The signal amplitude remains $A$ (deterministic). The RMS noise amplitude is now $\sigma/\sqrt{N}$. $$SNR_N = \frac{A}{\sigma / \sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \frac{A}{\sigma} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_1$$ $$\boxed{SNR_N = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_1}$$ **Conclusion:** The SNR grows as the square root of the number of averaged trials. This is the foundational engineering result of all evoked potential recording. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Trials Required for a Target SNR Gain > **Goal:** Given an initial $SNR_1$, find $N$ to achieve a target SNR ratio $K = SNR_N / SNR_1$. Starting from: $$K = \frac{SNR_N}{SNR_1} = \sqrt{N}$$ Squaring both sides: $$K^2 = N$$ $$\boxed{N = K^2}$$ **Numerical example (KTU exam-style):** A VEP signal has $SNR_1 = 0.1$. The clinician wants a usable trace with $SNR_N = 1.0$. $$K = \frac{1.0}{0.1} = 10 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N = 10^2 = 100 \text{ trials}$$ To achieve $SNR_N = 2.0$: $$K = 20 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N = 400 \text{ trials}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Central Conduction Time (CCT) for Somatosensory EP > **Goal:** Derive the conduction time from the cervico-medullary junction to the primary somatosensory cortex. **Step 1 — Identify two latencies:** - $L_{N13}$ — cervical response at C2 (dorsal column nucleus, lower medulla). - $L_{N20}$ — cortical response at C3'/C4' (primary somatosensory cortex). **Step 2 — Subtract to isolate the central segment:** $$CCT = L_{N20} - L_{N13}$$ **Step 3 — Clinical normal values (adult, upper limb median nerve SSEP):** - $L_{N13} \leq 14$ ms - $L_{N20} \leq 22$ ms - $\therefore CCT \leq 8$ ms **Step 4 — Clinical interpretation:** - Prolonged CCT → demyelination in the central somatosensory pathway (e.g., MS, cervical myelopathy). --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Brainstem Auditory EP — The I–V Interpeak Latency The BAEP has five Jewett waves: **I, II, III, IV, V**, generated at: - I → Distal auditory nerve (porus acusticus) - II → Proximal auditory nerve / cochlear nucleus - III → Pons (superior olivary complex) - IV → Midbrain (lateral lemniscus) - V → Inferior colliculus (thalamic input) **Central Brainstem Conduction Time (CBCT):** $$CBCT = L_V - L_{III} \leq 2.5 \text{ ms (adult)}$$ **Total I–V IPL (adults):** $$I{-}V \leq 4.6 \text{ ms}$$ Prolonged I–V with normal I-III suggests a **retrocochlear lesion** (e.g., acoustic neuroma at the cerebellopontine angle). --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: VEP P100 Latency Asymmetry Index > **Goal:** Quantify asymmetry between the two eyes. $$AI = \frac{\vert L_{OD} - L_{OS} \vert}{L_{OD} + L_{OS}} \times 100\%$$ Normal $AI \leq 8\%$. An $AI > 10\%$ indicates **unilateral conduction delay** (e.g., optic neuritis in one eye). --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Modality at a Glance | Feature | VEP | BAEP | SSEP | MEP | |---------|-----|------|------|-----| | Stimulus | Pattern reversal | Click | Electrical pulse | TMS | | Recording site | Occipital scalp | Vertex–earlobe | Parietal/Cervical | Muscle | | Key wave | **P100** | **V** (and I–III–V) | **N20**, **P37** | Compound muscle action potential | | Normal latency | $\leq$ 117 ms | $\leq$ 6.5 ms (peak V) | $\leq$ 22 ms (N20) | Variable | | Pathology delay | Demyelination | Acoustic neuroma | MS, cord lesion | Motor tract lesion | | Filter (Hz) | 1 – 100 | 100 – 3000 | 30 – 500 | 30 – 1500 | | Avg sweeps | 100 – 200 | 1000 – 2000 | 200 – 500 | Single trial | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — EP Recording System (Mechatronic Loop) ```mermaid flowchart LR Stim[Stimulus Generator
Pattern, Click, Pulse] -->|Physical Stimulus| Patient[Patient
Neural Pathway] Patient -->|Microvolt Bioelectric Signal| Electrode[Surface Electrodes
10-20 System] Electrode --> Amp[Differential Amplifier
Gain 10^5 to 10^6] Amp --> Filter[Bandpass Filter
1 Hz to 3 kHz] Filter --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter
Sampling > 1 kHz] ADC --> Trigger[Trigger Sync
Time-Lock to Stimulus] Trigger --> Avg[Signal Averager
Summing N Trials] Avg --> Display[Display
Waveform + Latency] Display --> Clinician[Clinician Interpretation] Clinician -->|Adjust Parameters| Stim ``` **Mechatronic interpretation:** The stimulus generator is the **actuator**, the patient is the **plant**, the electrodes are the **sensors**, the amplifier/filter/ADC is the **signal conditioner**, the averager is the **digital controller**, and the display closes the **human–machine interface loop**. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Signal Averaging Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Stimulus Trigger Pulse] --> B[Start ADC Sweep
Window: pre-stim to post-stim] B --> C[Acquire Single Trial x_i t] C --> D[Add to Running Sum
SUM x_i over N trials] D --> E{Count >= N ?} E -- No --> F[Wait Inter-stimulus Interval] F --> A E -- Yes --> G[Divide Sum by N
Compute Average] G --> H[Output Averaged EP Waveform] H --> I[Mark Latency Peaks P100, N20, V] I --> J[Clinical Report] ``` --- ## 4.3 Generator-Site Map of BAEP Waves ```mermaid flowchart TB Cochlea[Hair Cells of Cochlea] -->|I| N1[Wave I: Distal CN VIII] N1 -->|II| N2[Wave II: Proximal CN VIII / Cochlear Nucleus] N2 -->|III| N3[Wave III: Superior Olivary Complex, Pons] N3 -->|IV| N4[Wave IV: Lateral Lemniscus, Midbrain] N4 -->|V| N5[Wave V: Inferior Colliculus, Thalamus] N5 -->|VI-VII| N6[Wave VI-VII: Thalamocortical Radiations] ``` --- ## 4.4 SSEP Pathway — Stimulus to Cortex ```mermaid flowchart LR Stim[Electrical Stimulus
Median Nerve at Wrist] --> PN[Peripheral Nerve
Afferent Fibers] PN --> BrachPlex[Brachial Plexus] BrachPlex --> DRG[Dorsal Root Ganglion] DRG --> DC[Dorsal Column
Cuneate Fasciculus] DC --> Nucleus[Nucleus Cuneatus
Lower Medulla] Nucleus --> ML[Medial Lemniscus
Decussation] ML --> Thalamus[VPL Thalamus] Thalamus --> Cortex[Primary Somatosensory Cortex
Post-central Gyrus] Cortex --> N20[Recorded N20 Response
C3 prime or C4 prime] ``` --- ## 4.5 Decision Flow — Clinical Interpretation of an Abnormal EP ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[EP Waveform Acquired] --> CheckAmp[Check Amplitude] CheckAmp -->|Reduced| Axonal[Axonal Loss Pattern
Consider Neuropathy, Compression] CheckAmp -->|Normal| CheckLat[Check Latency] CheckLat -->|Prolonged| Demyel[Demyelination Pattern
Consider MS, GBS, Hereditary] CheckLat -->|Normal| CheckMorph[Check Morphology] CheckMorph -->|Desynchronized| Sync[Poor Synchrony
Consider Tremor, Artifact, Pediatric] CheckMorph -->|Normal| Normal[Report as Within Normal Limits] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define Evoked Potentials. List the three major clinical types and the primary neural pathway each assesses. **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **Evoked Potential (EP)** is the time-locked electrical response of the nervous system to a specific external stimulus, recorded from scalp or peripheral electrodes and extracted from background EEG noise by **signal averaging** (Dawson, 1947). The three major types are: 1. **Visual Evoked Potential (VEP)** — assesses the retinocalcarine pathway, generated by pattern-reversal checkerboard stimulation; key wave is the **P100** from the primary visual cortex. 2. **Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential (BAEP/BERA)** — assesses the auditory pathway from cochlea through the brainstem; key waves are Jewett's **I, III, V**. 3. **Somatosensory Evoked Potential (SSEP)** — assesses the dorsal column–medial lemniscus pathway from peripheral nerve to primary somatosensory cortex; key waves are **N20** (upper limb) and **P37** (lower limb). *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three types with their pathways.)* --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`** Why is signal averaging essential in evoked potential recording? State the SNR improvement law. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Evoked potentials are buried in the much larger background EEG noise (50–100 µV) because the EP amplitude is only **0.1–10 µV**. In a single trial, the EP is invisible. Signal averaging exploits the fact that the **EP is time-locked** to the stimulus (deterministic, reproducible) while the **noise is random and uncorrelated** with the stimulus. By acquiring $N$ trials and averaging them point-by-point, the noise variance reduces by $N$ while the signal amplitude is preserved, yielding: $$SNR_N = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_1$$ This is the **square-root law of averaging**. *(1 mark for the necessity reason, 1 mark for the principle of time-locking, 1 mark for the formula.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (Choice 1) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** **(a)** Describe the origin and recording methodology of **Visual Evoked Potentials (VEP)**. With a neat diagram, explain the international 10–20 electrode placement for VEP. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential (BAEP)** waveform, label the Jewett waves I to V with their anatomical generators, and discuss the clinical significance of the **I–V interpeak latency**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **VEP Origin and Recording (5 marks):** 1. **Stimulus:** A high-contrast **black-and-white checkerboard** pattern (or flash for uncooperative patients) reverses at 1–2 Hz. Each reversal is a stimulus event. 2. **Visual Pathway Tested:** Retinal ganglion cells → optic nerve → optic chiasm → optic tract → lateral geniculate nucleus (thalamus) → optic radiations → primary visual cortex (occipital lobe, Brodmann area 17). 3. **Electrodes (10–20 system):** - **Active electrode:** **Oz** (occipital midline, 3 cm above inion). - **Reference electrode:** **Fz** (frontal midline). - **Ground electrode:** Forehead (Fpz). 4. **Amplifier Settings:** Gain 10⁵, bandpass **1 – 100 Hz**, sampling rate ≥ 1 kHz. 5. **Averaging:** Typically 100–200 sweeps. 6. **Key Wave:** The major positive peak at **~100 ms** is the **P100**, generated in the primary visual cortex (striate cortex). 7. **Normal P100 latency:** ≤ **117 ms** in adults; interocular asymmetry ≤ **8 ms**. **Diagram (2 marks):** Block diagram of VEP setup — checkerboard → eye → optic nerve → LGN → visual cortex → Oz electrode → amplifier → averager → display. > [!NOTE] **Key Marks Tip:** Always state the **stimulus, electrode positions, filter settings, and the P100 latency** — these are the four examiner checkpoints. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **BAEP Waveform and Generators (5 marks):** | Wave | Generator | Approx. Latency | |------|-----------|-----------------| | I | Distal auditory nerve (porus acusticus internus) | 1.5 ms | | II | Proximal auditory nerve / cochlear nucleus | 2.5 ms | | III | Superior olivary complex (lower pons) | 3.5 ms | | IV | Lateral lemniscus (midbrain) | 4.5 ms | | V | Inferior colliculus (rostral midbrain, thalamic input) | 5.5 ms | **Recording Setup:** Vertex-active (Cz) referenced to earlobe (A1/A2) ipsilateral to click stimulus; click rate 10–30 Hz, 60–70 dB SL; contralateral ear masked with white noise; bandpass 100–3000 Hz; **1000–2000 trials averaged.** **Clinical Significance of I–V IPL (2 marks):** - **I–V interpeak latency** represents the **central brainstem conduction time** from auditory nerve to midbrain. - **Normal adult:** ≤ **4.6 ms**. - **Prolonged I–V IPL with normal I–III IPL** → retrocochlear lesion in the **cerebellopontine angle (CPA)**, e.g., **vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma)**. - **Prolonged I–III IPL** → lower brainstem lesion (e.g., MS plaque in pons). - **Absent waves** → severe auditory pathway disruption; absent wave V is a sign of profound hearing loss in neonates (BERA screening). --- ### Question B (Choice 2) **`[KTU University Exam - June 2022]`** **(a)** What are the **three clinical parameters** used to interpret evoked potentials? Explain how each parameter is affected in **demyelinating vs. axonal disease**, with one example for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a block diagram, describe the components of an **evoked potential recording system**. Derive the **square-root law of SNR improvement** with averaging. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **The Three Clinical Parameters (3 marks):** 1. **Latency (ms):** Time from stimulus onset to a specific peak (e.g., P100, N20, V). 2. **Amplitude (µV):** Peak-to-trough voltage difference of a specific wave. 3. **Morphology (waveform shape):** Indicates synchrony of neural firing. **Effect of Pathology (4 marks):** | Parameter | Demyelination (e.g., MS, GBS) | Axonal Loss (e.g., peripheral neuropathy, nerve transection) | |-----------|-------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------| | Latency | **Prolonged** (slowed saltatory conduction) | Usually normal (or mildly prolonged) | | Amplitude | Normal or mildly reduced | **Markedly reduced** (fewer functional axons) | | Morphology | May be dispersed | Often normal in remaining fibers | **Example for Demyelination:** Multiple Sclerosis — VEP P100 latency prolonged (often > 130 ms) due to demyelination of the optic nerve. **Example for Axonal Loss:** Diabetic peripheral neuropathy — SSEP N20 amplitude reduced while latency is near-normal due to loss of large myelinated fibers. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Same as Section 4.1 — Stimulus Generator → Patient → Electrodes → Differential Amplifier (Gain 10⁵) → Bandpass Filter → ADC → Signal Averager → Display. **Derivation of Square-Root Law (4 marks):** Full derivation as in Section 3.1, ending with: $$SNR_N = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_1$$ with clear stepwise justification (zero-mean noise assumption, variance additivity, signal coherence). > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing "latency" with "amplitude"** in clinical interpretation — 2-mark loss if the demyelination vs. axonal pattern is reversed. > 2. **Forgetting the "time-locked" qualifier** in the EP definition — this is the single most important defining property; examiners will deduct a full mark. > 3. **Omitting the units (ms, µV, Hz)** in numerical answers — KTU strictly penalizes unitless answers in 14-mark questions. > 4. **Missing the surface electrode placement** (Oz, Cz, C3') in VEP/BAEP/SSEP — examiners expect 10–20 system notation. > 5. **Skipping derivation steps in the SNR law** — writing only the final formula without the zero-mean/variance additivity reasoning costs 2 of 4 marks. > 6. **Mixing up Jewett waves** — wave I is the **distal auditory nerve**, not cochlea; wave V is **inferior colliculus**, not cortex. Memorize the column. > 7. **Confusing SSEP N20 with P37** — N20 is for **upper limb** (median nerve), P37 is for **lower limb** (tibial nerve). Examiners love this trap. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Evoked Potential = stimulus-locked, time-averaged, microvolt-range** neural response. - [ ] **Three modalities** (VEP, BAEP, SSEP) + MEP; each assesses a specific sensory pathway. - [ ] **SNR law:** $SNR_N = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_1$ → to double SNR, quadruple trials. - [ ] **Demyelination → ↑ latency, normal amplitude.** **Axonal loss → ↓ amplitude, normal latency.** - [ ] **VEP:** Stimulus = pattern reversal checkerboard; key wave = **P100** at Oz; normal ≤ **117 ms**. - [ ] **BAEP / BERA:** Stimulus = click; record Cz–A1/A2; key waves = Jewett **I, III, V**; I–V IPL ≤ **4.6 ms**. - [ ] **BAEP Jewett generators:** I = distal CN VIII; III = pons; V = inferior colliculus. - [ ] **SSEP upper limb:** Stimulus = median nerve at wrist; record N20 at C3'/C4' (contralateral parietal); normal ≤ **22 ms**. - [ ] **SSEP lower limb:** Stimulus = tibial nerve at ankle; record P37 at Cz'. - [ ] **Central Conduction Time (CCT)** for SSEP = $L_{N20} - L_{N13} \leq 8$ ms. - [ ] **Filter settings:** VEP 1–100 Hz, BAEP 100–3000 Hz, SSEP 30–500 Hz. - [ ] **Trials averaged:** VEP 100–200, BAEP 1000–2000, SSEP 200–500. - [ ] **10–20 system** electrode placements: Oz, Fz, Cz, C3', C4', A1, A2, Fpz. - [ ] **Clinical use cases:** MS diagnosis (VEP P100 delay), acoustic neuroma (prolonged I–V IPL), spinal cord injury (SSEP/MEP), IONM, neonatal hearing screening, BCI. - [ ] **Interpeak Latency (IPL)** = $L_2 - L_1$ — measures conduction between two neural generators. - [ ] **Conduction velocity** = $CV = D / \Delta t$ — converts distance and latency to speed. - [ ] **Dawson (1947)** introduced photographic superimposition; digital averaging replaced it in the 1960s. - [ ] **Interocular P100 asymmetry** > 8–10 ms is pathological (e.g., unilateral optic neuritis). - [ ] **CMRR of EP amplifiers** must be ≥ **80 dB** to reject 50/60 Hz mains interference. - [ ] **Skin–electrode impedance** must be kept below **5 kΩ** for clean recordings.

topics.title: 10-20 Electrodes Placement System for EEG - EEG Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT864 - BIOMECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** 10-20 Electrodes Placement System for EEG - EEG Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electroencephalography (EEG) — Formal Definition

**Electroencephalography (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique used to record the electrical activity of the human brain. It works by placing multiple electrodes on the scalp surface, which detect the tiny voltage fluctuations (in the order of **microvolts, $\mu$V**, typically $\mathbf{10 \text{ to } 100\,\mu V}$) generated by the synchronized ionic current flow within the neurons of the brain, especially the pyramidal cells of the cerebral cortex.

The recorded traces of brain electrical potentials over time constitute an **electroencephalogram**, and the device used to capture, amplify, filter, and display these signals is called an **EEG Machine** (or electroencephalograph).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus of this module is on the *placement strategy* (10-20 system) and the *hardware architecture* of an EEG machine — both of which are essential to extract clinically and diagnostically meaningful brain signals.

---

## 1.2 The 10-20 Electrode Placement System

The **International 10-20 System** is the standardized method recommended by the **International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology (IFCN)** for positioning scalp electrodes during an EEG recording. It was developed by **H. H. Jasper in 1958** and is the universally accepted protocol in clinical, research, and BCI (Brain-Computer Interface) applications.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Head as a Globe"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine the human head as the surface of the Earth (a globe). Just as we draw the **equator (a circumferential line through ear-to-ear)** and **meridians (longitudinal lines from front-to-back through the vertex)** to define any geographical point on Earth, the 10-20 system uses four anatomical landmarks (nasion, inion, and the two pre-auricular points) to draw imaginary **latitudes and longitudes** on the scalp. The intersection of these lines is where electrodes are placed. The name "10-20" comes from the fact that the inter-electrode distances are either **10%** or **20%** of specific skull reference measurements.

### The Four Key Anatomical Landmarks

| Landmark | Description | Position |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Nasion (Nz)** | Front-most midline point | Indentation at the bridge of the nose |
| **Inion (Iz)** | Back-most midline point | Bony prominence at the back of the skull |
| **Left Pre-auricular (LPA / A1)** | Left ear-front | Depression in front of the left ear |
| **Right Pre-auricular (RPA / A2)** | Right ear-front | Depression in front of the right ear |

The head circumference is measured between **Nz and Iz** through the **vertex (Cz)**, and the longitudinal distance is measured between the two **pre-auricular points** through **Cz**. The skull reference points and key electrode positions are illustrated later in the diagram section.

### Naming Convention for Electrode Sites

Each electrode site is given an alphabetic code based on the underlying **lobe** of the brain, plus a numeric suffix for laterality:

| Letter Code | Brain Region | Example |
|-------------|-------------|---------|
| **Fp** | Frontal Pole | Fp1, Fp2 |
| **F** | Frontal Lobe | F3, F4, F7, F8 |
| **T** | Temporal Lobe | T3, T4, T5, T6 |
| **C** | Central (Rolandic Fissure) | C3, C4, Cz |
| **P** | Parietal Lobe | P3, P4, Pz |
| **O** | Occipital Lobe | O1, O2 |
| **A** | Auricular (ear reference) | A1, A2 |

**Numeric Rule:**
- **Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7)** → **Left hemisphere** of the brain.
- **Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8)** → **Right hemisphere** of the brain.
- **"z" suffix** → **Midline (zero)** electrode (e.g., Fz, Cz, Pz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard clinical EEG uses **21 electrodes** (as per the 10-20 system), while high-density research and BCI systems may use **32, 64, 128, or 256 channels**, where additional electrodes are placed at **10%** intervals in between standard sites.

---

## 1.3 Standard Frequencies of EEG Rhythms

The brain produces rhythmic oscillations, classified by their frequency bands. These rhythms are clinically significant and are central to KTU examination questions.

| Rhythm | Frequency Band | Amplitude | Location | Associated State |
|--------|---------------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **Delta (δ)** | $0.5 \text{ to } 4\,\text{Hz}$ | High ($\sim 20\text{–}200\,\mu V$) | Cortex (adult), deep sleep | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, infants |
| **Theta (θ)** | $4 \text{ to } 8\,\text{Hz}$ | Moderate | Temporal, parietal | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation |
| **Alpha (α)** | $8 \text{ to } 13\,\text{Hz}$ | Moderate ($\sim 20\text{–}60\,\mu V$) | Occipital, parietal | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed |
| **Beta (β)** | $13 \text{ to } 30\,\text{Hz}$ | Low | Frontal, central | Active thinking, alertness, focus |
| **Gamma (γ)** | $> 30\,\text{Hz}$ | Very low | Various | Cognitive processing, perception |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Placement Logic of the 10-20 System

The placement procedure follows a strict, reproducible geometric algorithm. Below is the structured logic that every KTU student must memorize.

### Step 1 — Measure the Head
- **Head Circumference (C):** Distance from Nasion (Nz) → Inion (Iz) passing over the vertex (Cz).
- **Head Longitudinal Distance (L):** Distance from Left Pre-Auricular (LPA) → Right Pre-Auricular (RPA) passing over Cz.

### Step 2 — Compute Reference Distances
The standard intervals used for placing electrodes are:

$$d_{\text{10}} = 10\% \times C \quad \text{(for lateral placements)}$$

$$d_{\text{20}} = 20\% \times C \quad \text{(for primary sagittal placements)}$$

### Step 3 — Place Midline (z) Electrodes
Starting at **Nz**, the midline electrodes are placed at **10% of C** from Nz:
- **Fpz** = 10% of C from Nz
- **Fz** = 20% of C from Nz (= 30% from Inion)
- **Cz** = 50% of C from Nz (the vertex)
- **Pz** = 20% of C from Inion
- **Oz** = 10% of C from Inion

### Step 4 — Place Coronal (Left-Right) Lines
A coronal circle is drawn through **Fp1, F7, T3, T5, O1** (and the corresponding right-hemisphere sites), and another through **Fp2, F8, T4, T6, O2**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The geometric uniformity of the 10-20 system ensures **reproducibility** across patients, clinicians, and laboratories. For BCI applications, this is critical because the signal processing pipelines (e.g., Common Spatial Patterns) rely on consistent electrode coordinates.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle of an EEG Machine

The EEG machine is a high-gain, low-noise, multi-channel bioelectric amplifier system. Its block-level architecture is shown in the diagram section. The functional signal flow is:

1. **Electrode–Electrolyte Interface:** The metal electrode and the conductive electrolytic gel form a **half-cell potential**. A pair of electrodes measures the **potential difference** between two scalp sites.
2. **Input Protection & DC Blocking:** Passive resistor-capacitor (RC) networks remove DC offset from the half-cell potential and protect the input from electrostatic discharge (ESD).
3. **Differential Amplifier (Instrumentation Amplifier):** Provides high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR > 100 dB)** and amplifies the tiny difference signal (typically $\sim 10\text{–}100\,\mu V$) by a gain of $\sim 10^3\text{–}10^4$.
4. **Band-Pass Filtering:** Typically **0.5 Hz to 70 Hz** (clinical EEG) or **0.1 Hz to 100 Hz** (research EEG), to remove DC drift and high-frequency noise.
5. **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz):** A narrow band-reject filter removes power-line interference.
6. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Sampling at **$f_s \geq 256 \text{ Hz}$** (Nyquist requirement: $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{\max}$).
7. **Display / Storage:** Real-time waveform display, spectral analysis, and digital storage.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Equations

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Midline electrode position | $x = n \times 0.10 \times C$ | $C$ = head circumference (cm); $n$ = integer | $n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ for Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz |
| 2 | EEG signal amplitude | $V_{\text{EEG}} \in [10, 100]\,\mu V$ | $\mu V$ | Typical range at scalp |
| 3 | Half-cell potential (Nernst) | $E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}\right)$ | $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K); $T$ = 310 K; $F$ = 96485 C/mol; $n$ = valency | Electrode–electrolyte interface |
| 4 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ (in dB) | $A_d$ = differential gain; $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain | $CMRR > 100\,\text{dB}$ required for EEG |
| 5 | Nyquist Sampling Criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | $f_s$ = sampling freq; $f_{\max}$ = highest signal freq | For $f_{\max} = 70\,\text{Hz}$, use $f_s \geq 256\,\text{Hz}$ |
| 6 | Skin–Electrode Impedance | $Z_{\text{skin}} < 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $\text{k}\Omega$ | Standard clinical limit |
| 7 | Noise voltage (thermal, Johnson) | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K | Sets the noise floor of the amplifier |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The same signal acquisition principles are used in **ECG, EMG, EOG, and ECoG** (electrocorticography). The 10-20 system is the foundation of modern **BCIs** for applications such as prosthetic limb control, neurofeedback, and seizure detection.

---

## 2.4 Differential Measurement: Why Two Electrodes Are Needed

A single scalp electrode only measures the **half-cell potential** at the skin-electrolyte junction, which is an unwanted DC offset. To recover the true neural signal, **two electrodes** are connected to a **differential amplifier**:

$$V_{\text{out}} = A_d \cdot (V_+ - V_-) + A_{cm} \cdot \left(\frac{V_+ + V_-}{2}\right)$$

For an ideal amplifier, $A_{cm} = 0$, so $V_{\text{out}} = A_d \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$. A third electrode, the **driven right leg (DRL)** or **ground electrode**, is connected to the body to bias the patient to a known potential and improve safety.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Determining Electrode Coordinates on a Given Head

### Problem Setup
A patient's head circumference is measured as $C = 56\,\text{cm}$ and the longitudinal distance (ear-to-ear over vertex) is $L = 36\,\text{cm}$. Determine the **arc-length positions** of all midline (z) electrodes: Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz, measured from the nasion.

### Step 1 — Recall the placement rule
The midline electrodes are spaced at 10% intervals of the total head circumference $C$, starting from the nasion (Nz). Therefore, the position of the $k$-th electrode from the nasion is:

$$x_k = k \times 0.10 \times C \quad \text{where } k = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$$

### Step 2 — Compute 10% of C
$$0.10 \times C = 0.10 \times 56\,\text{cm} = 5.6\,\text{cm}$$

### Step 3 — Apply to each electrode

For **Fpz** ($k=1$):
$$x_{Fpz} = 1 \times 0.10 \times 56 = 5.6\,\text{cm} \text{ from Nz}$$

For **Fz** ($k=2$):
$$x_{Fz} = 2 \times 0.10 \times 56 = 11.2\,\text{cm} \text{ from Nz}$$

For **Cz** ($k=3$):
$$x_{Cz} = 3 \times 0.10 \times 56 = 16.8\,\text{cm} \text{ from Nz}$$

For **Pz** ($k=4$):
$$x_{Pz} = 4 \times 0.10 \times 56 = 22.4\,\text{cm} \text{ from Nz}$$

For **Oz** ($k=5$):
$$x_{Oz} = 5 \times 0.10 \times 56 = 28.0\,\text{cm} \text{ from Nz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that the nasion-to-inion arc should equal 50% of $C$ at Cz, which is consistent: $x_{Cz} = 28\,\text{cm} = 50\% \times 56\,\text{cm} = 28\,\text{cm}$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Sampling Rate for an EEG Machine

### Step 1 — Identify the highest signal frequency
The clinical EEG bandwidth is **0.5 Hz to 70 Hz**, so $f_{\max} = 70\,\text{Hz}$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem
To avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency must satisfy:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the theoretical minimum
$$f_{s,\min} = 2 \times 70\,\text{Hz} = 140\,\text{Hz}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the engineering safety factor
In practice, an oversampling factor of $\sim 1.8$–$2$ is used to allow for realizable anti-aliasing analog filter roll-off. The KTU-accepted standard for clinical EEG is:

$$f_s = 256\,\text{Hz (clinical)} \quad \text{or} \quad 512\,\text{Hz (research)}$$

### Step 5 — Verify against Nyquist
$$f_s = 256\,\text{Hz} \geq 2 \times 70\,\text{Hz} = 140\,\text{Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Required CMRR to Reject 50 Hz Power-Line Interference

### Step 1 — Define the problem
A differential amplifier has a differential gain $A_d = 10{,}000$ and an unwanted common-mode gain of $A_{cm} = 1$. The common-mode input from mains interference is $V_{cm} = 2\,\text{V}$ (at 50 Hz) on each input. The desired EEG signal of interest is $V_d = 50\,\mu V$.

### Step 2 — Compute the CMRR
$$CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{10{,}000}{1}\right)$$

$$CMRR = 20 \times 4 = 80\,\text{dB}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the common-mode output noise
$$V_{\text{out,cm}} = A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm} = 1 \times 2\,\text{V} = 2\,\text{V} \text{ (unacceptably large!)}$$

### Step 4 — Note the engineering conclusion
This is why **EEG instrumentation amplifiers** must have a CMRR $> 100\,\text{dB}$. At 100 dB:

$$\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} = 10^5 \Rightarrow A_{cm} = \frac{10{,}000}{10^5} = 0.1$$

$$V_{\text{out,cm}} = 0.1 \times 2\,\text{V} = 200\,\text{mV (still significant!)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The full $V_{cm}$ does not appear at the inputs because the body acts as a resistor divider. The body-site common-mode voltage is typically $< 1\,\text{mV}$, so the residual interference is $\sim 0.1\,\mu V$ — well below the EEG band. This is why **DRL circuits and CMRR $> 100\,\text{dB}$** are both essential.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Skin–Electrode Contact Impedance and Noise

Using the Johnson-Nyquist noise equation:

$$V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$$

### Step 1 — Plug in values
For a skin–electrode impedance $R = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, body temperature $T = 310\,\text{K}$, and EEG bandwidth $\Delta f = 70\,\text{Hz}$:

$$V_n = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 \times 5000 \times 70}$$

### Step 2 — Compute step-by-step
$$4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} = 5.52 \times 10^{-23}$$

$$5.52 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 = 1.7112 \times 10^{-20}$$

$$1.7112 \times 10^{-20} \times 5000 = 8.556 \times 10^{-17}$$

$$8.556 \times 10^{-17} \times 70 = 5.989 \times 10^{-15}$$

### Step 3 — Take the square root
$$V_n = \sqrt{5.989 \times 10^{-15}} \approx 7.74 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{V} = 0.077\,\mu V$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows the thermal noise from a $5\,\text{k}\Omega$ electrode contact is $\sim 0.08\,\mu V$, which is **below** the EEG signal amplitude. Thus, low-impedance electrodes (achieved by skin abrasion + conductive gel) are necessary for high-fidelity recordings.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Input / Given | Output / Resulted Quantity | Engineering Implication |
|---------|---------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| 10% rule (midline) | $C = 56\,\text{cm}$ | $Fpz = 5.6\,\text{cm}$ from Nz | Standardized placement |
| Nyquist criterion | $f_{\max} = 70\,\text{Hz}$ | $f_s \geq 256\,\text{Hz}$ | Anti-aliasing requirement |
| CMRR | $A_d/A_{cm} = 10^5$ | $CMRR = 100\,\text{dB}$ | Mains noise rejection |
| Thermal noise | $R = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $T = 310\,\text{K}$ | $V_n \approx 0.08\,\mu V$ | SNR remains high |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 10-20 Electrode Placement System (Top-Down Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Patient Head Top View] --> B[Draw Circumferential Lines]
    A --> C[Draw Longitudinal Lines]
    B --> D[Measure 10% and 20% Intervals]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Identify Midline z-Electrodes: Fpz Fz Cz Pz Oz]
    D --> F[Place Coronal Row: Fp1 Fp2]
    D --> G[Place Frontal Row: F3 F4 F7 F8]
    D --> H[Place Central Row: C3 C4]
    D --> I[Place Temporal Row: T3 T4 T5 T6]
    D --> J[Place Parietal Row: P3 P4]
    D --> K[Place Occipital Row: O1 O2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Schematic Note:** The above block-flow represents the algorithmic procedure a technician follows. The actual scalp map shows the electrodes arranged as concentric "rings" — Nasion-Inion forms one, and the ear-to-ear chain forms the other.

---

## 4.2 EEG Machine — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Scalp Electrodes] --> B[Electrode-Electrolyte Interface and DC Blocking RC]
    B --> C[Input Protection and Patient Safety Resistors]
    C --> D[Differential Instrumentation Amplifier]
    D --> E[Band-Pass Filter 0.5 to 70 Hz]
    E --> F[Notch Filter 50 or 60 Hz Mains Reject]
    F --> G[Further Gain Stage and Level Shifting]
    G --> H[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    H --> I[Microcontroller or DSP Processing Unit]
    I --> J[Display Monitor and Storage]
    I --> K[Spectral Analysis FFT Module]
    L[Driven Right Leg DRL Circuit] --> A
    M[Power Supply and Isolation Barrier] --> D
```

**Description of the flow (top-down and left-to-right):**
1. **Scalp Electrodes** pick up microvolt-level neural signals.
2. The **electrode-electrolyte interface** introduces a DC offset (half-cell potential), removed by an **RC high-pass filter**.
3. **Input protection** (typically current-limiting resistors and gas-discharge tubes or Zener clamps) safeguards the patient and amplifier from defibrillation or ESD.
4. The **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., AD620, INA128) provides high CMRR and initial gain.
5. The **band-pass filter** restricts the spectrum to clinically useful EEG bands.
6. The **notch filter** removes 50 Hz (India/Europe) or 60 Hz (USA) mains interference.
7. A second gain stage brings the signal into the ADC's input range (typically $\pm 5\,\text{V}$).
8. The **ADC** digitizes the signal at $f_s \geq 256\,\text{Hz}$.
9. The **DSP / Microcontroller** runs FFT, displays waveforms, and stores data.

---

## 4.3 Montage Reference Schemes (Comparison Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[EEG Reference Schemes] --> B[Bipolar Montage]
    A --> C[Referential Monopolar]
    A --> D[Average Reference]
    B --> B1[Uses chain of adjacent electrodes e.g. Fp1 F3 C3 P3 O1]
    C --> C1[All electrodes referenced to a single site e.g. A1 plus A2 or Cz]
    D --> D1[All electrodes referenced to the mean of all electrodes]
```

| Montage Type | Reference | Best Use | Notes |
|--------------|-----------|----------|-------|
| **Bipolar** | Adjacent electrode pairs in a chain | Localizing focal abnormalities | Excellent for phase reversals |
| **Referential (Monopolar)** | A single reference (ear A1/A2, or vertex Cz) | Amplitude measurements | Susceptible to reference contamination |
| **Average Reference** | Mean of all electrode potentials | Whole-head overview | Used in source localization and BCI |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the International 10-20 System of electrode placement. Mention any four anatomical landmarks used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The International 10-20 System is a standardized procedure developed by H. H. Jasper (1958) for placing EEG electrodes on the scalp such that inter-electrode distances are either 10% or 20% of specific head measurements. It ensures reproducibility across patients and laboratories.

**Four anatomical landmarks (1 mark each):**
1. **Nasion (Nz):** Anterior midline depression at the bridge of the nose.
2. **Inion (Iz):** Bony prominence at the back of the skull.
3. **Left Pre-Auricular Point (A1):** Depression in front of the left ear.
4. **Right Pre-Auricular Point (A2):** Depression in front of the right ear.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the five standard EEG rhythm bands with their frequency ranges and one associated state for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Rhythm | Frequency | Associated State |
|--------|-----------|------------------|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep (slow-wave) sleep |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness, light sleep |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | Active thinking, focus |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz | Higher cognitive processing |

(1 mark for frequency ranges, 1 mark for brain regions, 1 mark for states)

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an EEG machine with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of the differential amplifier and the importance of CMRR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a diagram, describe the 10-20 electrode placement system. Explain the naming convention of electrodes and the placement of midline (z) electrodes. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### (a) Working Principle of EEG Machine (7 marks)

An EEG machine records the minute electrical potentials generated by the brain's neuronal activity. The block diagram (refer to Section 4.2) shows the following stages:

1. **Electrodes (1 mark):** Ag/AgCl disc electrodes are placed on the scalp with conductive gel to ensure low contact impedance ($Z < 5\,\text{k}\Omega$).

2. **Differential Instrumentation Amplifier (2 marks):** Amplifies the potential difference between two scalp sites:
$$V_{\text{out}} = A_d \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$$

3. **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) (2 marks):** Defined as:
$$CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)\,\text{dB}$$

For EEG, $CMRR \geq 100\,\text{dB}$ is required to reject 50 Hz mains interference. Without adequate CMRR, the desired $50\,\mu V$ signal would be swamped by common-mode noise.

4. **Filtering, ADC, and Display (2 marks):** Band-pass filtering (0.5–70 Hz), notch filter (50 Hz), ADC ($f_s \geq 256\,\text{Hz}$), and real-time display/storage complete the chain.

#### (b) 10-20 Electrode Placement System (7 marks)

**Diagram (2 marks):** A top-view of the head with circumferential and longitudinal lines, showing the standard 21 electrode positions (Fpz, Fp1, Fp2, F7, F3, Fz, F4, F8, T3, C3, Cz, C4, T4, T5, P3, Pz, P4, T6, O1, Oz, O2).

**Naming Convention (2 marks):**
- **Letter** → underlying brain lobe (F, T, C, P, O, Fp).
- **Odd numbers** → left hemisphere; **even numbers** → right hemisphere.
- **"z" suffix** → midline.

**Midline Placement (3 marks):** Fpz at 10% of $C$ from Nz, Fz at 20%, Cz at 50% (vertex), Pz at 80%, Oz at 100% (inion). The reference head circumference $C$ is measured as the arc from nasion to inion through the vertex.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of bipolar and referential montages in EEG recording. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With proper derivations, determine: (i) the minimum sampling frequency required for an EEG machine with a bandwidth of 70 Hz, and (ii) the thermal noise voltage developed across a skin–electrode interface of 5 kΩ at body temperature (310 K) for a bandwidth of 70 Hz. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### (a) Montages in EEG (7 marks)

**Bipolar Montage (3 marks):** Each channel records the potential difference between two adjacent scalp electrodes (e.g., Fp1–F3, F3–C3, C3–P3, P3–O1).
- **Advantage:** Excellent for localizing focal abnormalities via phase reversals.
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot determine absolute amplitude.

**Referential (Monopolar) Montage (2 marks):** All electrodes are referenced to a single common electrode (e.g., A1+A2 linked ears, or vertex Cz).
- **Advantage:** True amplitude measurement is possible.
- **Disadvantage:** Reference site contamination affects all channels.

**Average Reference (2 marks):** Reference is the mean of all electrode signals. Reduces reference bias; used in BCI and source localization.

#### (b) Derivation Problems (7 marks)

**(i) Minimum Sampling Frequency (3 marks):**
By the Nyquist criterion:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max} = 2 \times 70\,\text{Hz} = 140\,\text{Hz}$$

For practical implementation with a realizable anti-aliasing filter, $f_s = 256\,\text{Hz}$ (clinical) is chosen.

**(ii) Thermal Noise (4 marks):** Using Johnson–Nyquist:
$$V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$$

Substituting $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$, $T = 310\,\text{K}$, $R = 5 \times 10^{3}\,\Omega$, $\Delta f = 70\,\text{Hz}$:

$$V_n = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 70}$$

$$V_n = \sqrt{5.99 \times 10^{-15}} \approx 7.74 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{V} = 0.077\,\mu V$$

This is well below EEG amplitudes ($10\text{–}100\,\mu V$), confirming low-impedance electrodes provide adequate SNR.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing **odd (left)** and **even (right)** electrode numbering. Loss of 1 mark per instance.
> 2. Forgetting to specify the **Nasion-Inion reference** in the 10-20 system. Mentioning only the percentages without reference is incomplete.
> 3. Writing **CMRR formula without units (dB)** — the term $\log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ multiplied by 20 yields dB; explicit mention is required.
> 4. For EEG sampling questions, students often write $f_s = 70\,\text{Hz}$, forgetting the **factor of 2** from Nyquist.
> 5. Failing to mention the **DRL (Driven Right Leg)** or **ground electrode** when asked about EEG safety — this is a frequent 1–2 mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **10-20 system** is a *standardized* placement system using 10% and 20% of head reference measurements.
- ✅ Four landmarks: **Nasion, Inion, LPA (A1), RPA (A2)**.
- ✅ Electrode naming: **letter (region) + number (laterality)** + optional **"z" (midline)**.
- ✅ **Odd = Left**, **Even = Right**, **z = Midline**.
- ✅ **EEG signal amplitude**: $10\text{–}100\,\mu V$.
- ✅ **CMRR** must exceed **100 dB** in EEG instrumentation amplifiers.
- ✅ **Bandwidth** for clinical EEG: **0.5 Hz to 70 Hz**.
- ✅ **Sampling rate**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max} = 140\,\text{Hz}$, but **256 Hz is the clinical standard**.
- ✅ **Skin–electrode impedance** must be **< 5 kΩ** for high-fidelity recording.
- ✅ The **DRL (Driven Right Leg)** circuit is essential for patient safety and noise reduction.
- ✅ **Five EEG rhythms**: Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, Gamma — with frequency bands $0.5$–$4$, $4$–$8$, $8$–$13$, $13$–$30$, $>30\,\text{Hz}$.
- ✅ **Montages**: Bipolar, Referential, Average Reference — each with distinct advantages.
- ✅ EEG machine signal chain: **Electrode → DC Block → Protection → Instrumentation Amplifier → BPF → Notch → ADC → DSP → Display**.
- ✅ **Nyquist theorem** governs ADC sampling; **Johnson noise** sets the noise floor of the input stage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Physiological Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT864 - BIOMECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Physiological Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Physiological Parameters** are the quantifiable, biophysical and biochemical variables generated by the functioning of living systems (human body) that characterize the state of health, homeostasis, and dynamic equilibrium of an organism. In the context of **Biomechatronics (PEBRT864)**, the *measurement of physiological parameters* refers to the systematic acquisition, transduction, conditioning, and display of biosignals such as the electrocardiogram (ECG), electromyogram (EMG), electroencephalogram (EEG), blood pressure, heart rate, body temperature, respiration rate, and blood oxygen saturation (SpO₂), using non-invasive or minimally invasive sensor–instrumentation chains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Physiological measurement is the bridge between *biology* (the signal source) and *mechatronics* (the engineering hardware — sensor, signal conditioner, ADC, microcontroller, actuator, display). Every measurement chain must preserve **signal fidelity**, ensure **patient safety** (galvanic isolation, leakage current < **10 µA** as per IEC 60601-1), and provide **clinical accuracy**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Body as a City"

Imagine the human body as a **vast, self-regulating city**:

- The **heart** is the power plant (generates electrical + mechanical signals — ECG, blood pressure, pulse).
