- The **brain** is the central command headquarters (generates low-level electrical chatter — EEG).
- The **muscles** are the robotic arms of the city (generate EMG currents when activated).
- The **lungs** are the air-conditioning system (respiration rate, SpO₂).
- The **skin** is the outer wall whose temperature reveals the city's internal heat balance (core/skin temperature).

A **biomechatronic measurement system** is essentially a *telemetry engineer* standing outside the city walls, using specialized microphones, thermometers, pressure gauges, and oscilloscopes to *listen in* on the city's internal activities — without disturbing it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All physiological signals share three properties: (1) **low amplitude** (µV to mV range), (2) **low frequency** (0.05 Hz to a few kHz), and (3) **susceptibility to noise** (50/60 Hz mains interference, motion artefacts, electrode-skin impedance). Hence *signal conditioning* is the heart of any biomedical instrument.

## 1.3 Classification of Physiological Parameters

| Class | Parameter | Typical Range | Sensor / Transducer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrical** | ECG | 0.5 – 5 mV | Ag/AgCl surface electrodes |
| **Electrical** | EEG | 5 – 300 µV | Scalp electrodes |
| **Electrical** | EMG | 50 µV – 5 mV | Surface / needle electrodes |
| **Mechanical** | Blood Pressure (BP) | 80/50 – 200/120 mmHg | Sphygmomanometer, strain gauge |
| **Mechanical** | Heart Rate (HR) | 40 – 200 bpm | PPG, ECG-derived |
| **Mechanical** | Respiration Rate | 8 – 40 breaths/min | Chest belt, nasal thermistor |
| **Thermal** | Core Temperature | 36.1 – 37.8 °C | Thermistor, RTD |
| **Optical** | SpO₂ | 70 – 100 % | Pulse oximeter (LED + photodiode) |
| **Chemical** | pH, Glucose, pO₂ | varies | Ion-selective electrodes |

## 1.4 Why Measurement Matters in Biomechatronics

In a closed-loop prosthetic limb, exoskeleton, or rehabilitation robot, **physiological feedback** is essential for:
1. **Intent decoding** (EMG → motor command).
2. **Closed-loop control** (e.g., functional electrical stimulation).
3. **Patient monitoring** (ICU, wearable IoT).
4. **Human–machine interfacing (HMI)** in brain–computer interfaces (BCI).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalized Biomedical Measurement Chain

Every physiological parameter measurement passes through a canonical chain:

1. **Signal Source (Biomedical Event):** The physiological process (e.g., cardiac depolarization).
2. **Sensor / Electrode / Transducer:** Converts the biological event into an electrical signal.
3. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, filtering, isolation, linearization.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Sampling (must obey **Nyquist theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$).
5. **Digital Processing & Display:** Microcontroller / DSP / PC.
6. **Actuator / Feedback (Mechatronic loop):** Motor, FES, alarm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Standard:** Medical instruments must comply with **IEC 60601-1**. Maximum allowed patient leakage current is **10 µA (CF type)** and **100 µA (BF type)**. The device must withstand a defibrillator discharge of **5 kV**.

## 2.2 Bioelectrodes — The Sensor Interface

Bioelectrodes transduce ionic currents in tissue into electronic currents in the measurement instrument.

- **Polarizable electrodes** (e.g., Pt): behave like a capacitor — no DC current flows across the electrode–electrolyte interface.
- **Non-polarizable electrodes** (e.g., **Ag/AgCl**): allow free current flow with a stable half-cell potential of approximately **+0.223 V** vs. SHE.

The **electrode–skin interface** is modelled as a half-cell potential $E_{hc}$ in series with a parallel RC network representing polarization impedance.

## 2.3 ECG — Electrocardiogram

The ECG records the electrical activity of the heart from the body surface using **10 electrodes** in a standard **12-lead configuration** (3 Einthoven, 3 augmented Goldberger, 6 precordial Wilson).

- **Einthoven's Triangle** uses electrodes on the **Right Arm (RA)**, **Left Arm (LA)**, and **Left Leg (LL)**.
- The three standard limb leads are:
  - **Lead I** = $V_{LA} - V_{RA}$
  - **Lead II** = $V_{LL} - V_{RA}$
  - **Lead III** = $V_{LL} - V_{LA}$

The **Einthoven relation** (KCL on the triangle) is:
$$ \text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III} = \text{Lead II} $$

## 2.4 EMG — Electromyogram

EMG records the sum of motor unit action potentials (MUAPs) during muscle contraction.

- **Surface EMG (sEMG):** non-invasive, low-pass filtered at ~**500 Hz**, high-pass at ~**20 Hz**, suitable for prosthetics.
- **Needle EMG (nEMG):** invasive, captures single MUAPs, used in clinical neurology.

## 2.5 EEG — Electroencephalogram

EEG measures postsynaptic cortical activity via the international **10–20 electrode placement system**.

| Band | Frequency (Hz) | Associated State |
|---|---|---|
| Delta (δ) | 0.5 – 4 | Deep sleep |
| Theta (θ) | 4 – 8 | Drowsiness |
| Alpha (α) | 8 – 13 | Relaxed, eyes closed |
| Beta (β) | 13 – 30 | Active thinking |
| Gamma (γ) | > 30 | Perception, cognition |

## 2.6 Blood Pressure Measurement

- **Auscultatory method** (mercury / aneroid sphygmomanometer + stethoscope): cuff inflated to > systolic, slowly deflated. Korotkoff sounds mark **SBP** and **DBP**.
- **Oscillometric method** (digital BP monitors): the cuff pressure oscillates as the artery pulses; mean arterial pressure (MAP) corresponds to the **maximum oscillation amplitude**.

## 2.7 Pulse Oximetry (SpO₂)

Uses **photoplethysmography (PPG)**: two wavelengths — **660 nm (red)** for deoxyhaemoglobin and **940 nm (infrared)** for oxyhaemoglobin. The ratio of AC to DC components of the transmitted light at each wavelength gives SpO₂.

$$ \text{SpO}_2 \approx 110 - 25 \cdot R \quad \text{where} \quad R = \frac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}} $$

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$ | Resistance of conductor (electrode/tissue) | Ω |
| 2 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling rate | Hz |
| 3 | $I + III = II$ | Einthoven's law | mV |
| 4 | $HR = \frac{60}{RR_{interval}}$ | Heart rate from ECG | bpm |
| 5 | $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ | Mean arterial pressure | mmHg |
| 6 | $Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P \, r^4}{8 \eta L}$ | Poiseuille flow (cardiac output basis) | m³/s |
| 7 | $R = \frac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$ | Ratio-of-ratios for SpO₂ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 9 | $\frac{1}{T} = A + B\ln R + C(\ln R)^3$ | Steinhart–Hart (thermistor) | Kelvin |
| 10 | $V_{oc} = E_{hc} \cdot A$ | Open-circuit voltage of bioelectrode | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** The same signal-chain architecture (sensor → IA → BPF → ADC → DSP) is reused across ECG, EMG, and EEG — only the **gain**, **bandwidth**, and **electrode placement** change. This modularity is the cornerstone of modern wearable health monitors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)

**Given:** A blood pressure cuff records systolic blood pressure (SBP) and diastolic blood pressure (DBP). The arterial pressure waveform is approximately periodic with a duty cycle: the heart spends ~**1/3** of the cardiac cycle in systole and **2/3** in diastole.

**Step 1 — Write the integral form of MAP over one cardiac period $T$:**
$$ MAP = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} P(t) \, dt $$

**Step 2 — Approximate the integral as a weighted average of SBP (during systole, fraction $1/3$) and DBP (during diastole, fraction $2/3$):**
$$ MAP \approx \frac{1}{3} SBP + \frac{2}{3} DBP $$

**Step 3 — Rearrange by factoring out DBP:**
$$ MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP) $$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** If a patient has SBP = 120 mmHg, DBP = 80 mmHg:
$$ MAP = 80 + \frac{1}{3}(120 - 80) = 80 + 13.33 \approx 93.33 \text{ mmHg} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is the most frequently tested derivation in KTU biomechatronics papers for blood-pressure modules.

## 3.2 Derivation of Heart Rate from RR Interval

**Given:** The ECG waveform contains a clearly identifiable **R-peak** in the QRS complex. Let the time between two consecutive R-peaks be $RR$ seconds.

**Step 1 — Number of beats in 1 second:** $\frac{1}{RR}$

**Step 2 — Convert to beats per minute (multiply by 60):**
$$ HR = \frac{60}{RR_{interval}} \quad [\text{bpm}] $$

**Step 3 — Example:** If $RR = 0.8 \text{ s}$,
$$ HR = \frac{60}{0.8} = 75 \text{ bpm} $$

## 3.3 Derivation of Sampling Rate (Nyquist) for ECG

**Given:** The highest significant frequency component of an ECG signal is approximately **150 Hz** (QRS complex contains frequencies up to ~150 Hz; clinical ECG bandwidth is **0.05 – 150 Hz**).

**Step 1 — Apply Nyquist theorem:**
$$ f_s \geq 2 f_{max} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute $f_{max} = 150$ Hz:**
$$ f_s \geq 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ Hz} $$

**Step 3 — Practical engineering choice (oversampling factor 3 – 5× to ease anti-alias filter design):**
$$ f_s \approx 360 \text{ Hz} \quad \text{or} \quad 500 \text{ Hz} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students frequently confuse **sampling rate** with **signal bandwidth**. The signal *bandwidth* is 0.05 – 150 Hz; the *sampling rate* must be ≥ **300 Hz** at the ADC.

## 3.4 Derivation of Cardiac Output via Poiseuille Approximation (Conceptual)

**Given:** For a single large vessel approximated as a rigid tube, Poiseuille's law gives the volumetric flow $Q$ as:
$$ Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P \, r^4}{8 \eta L} $$

where $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop, $r$ is the vessel radius, $\eta$ is the blood viscosity (≈ **3.5 cP**), and $L$ is the vessel length.

**Step 1 — Multiply by heart rate $HR$ to get cardiac output (per minute):**
$$ CO = HR \times Q $$

**Step 2 — Approximate the aorta with $r = 0.012 \text{ m}$, $L = 0.4 \text{ m}$, $\Delta P \approx 100 \text{ mmHg} = 13330 \text{ Pa}$, $\eta = 0.0035 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $HR = 70 \text{ bpm}$:**

$$ Q = \frac{\pi \times 13330 \times (0.012)^4}{8 \times 0.0035 \times 0.4} $$

$$ Q = \frac{\pi \times 13330 \times 2.0736 \times 10^{-8}}{0.0112} $$

$$ Q = \frac{8.681 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0112} \approx 0.0775 \text{ L/s} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to L/min:**
$$ CO = 70 \times 0.0775 \times 60 \approx 325.5 \text{ L/min} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This value is higher than the true cardiac output (~5 L/min) because the **aorta is not a rigid tube** and blood is non-Newtonian. The Poiseuille derivation is a *first-order teaching approximation* — in clinical practice, the **Fick principle** or **thermodilution** is used.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Key Physiological Measurements

| Parameter | Sensor | Bandwidth | Amplitude | Key Noise Source | KTU Mark-Worth Fact |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ECG | Ag/AgCl | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 0.5 – 5 mV | 50 Hz mains | $I + III = II$ |
| EMG | Surface/needle | 20 – 500 Hz | 50 µV – 5 mV | Motion artefact | Used in prosthetics |
| EEG | Scalp | 0.5 – 100 Hz | 5 – 300 µV | Eye blinks, EMG | 10–20 system |
| BP (NIBP) | Cuff + strain gauge | DC – 5 Hz | 0 – 200 mmHg | Cuff size error | MAP = DBP + ⅓ PP |
| SpO₂ | Red + IR LED | 0.5 – 5 Hz | 70 – 100 % | Ambient light, motion | Ratio-of-ratios (R) |
| Temperature | Thermistor | DC | 36 – 42 °C | Self-heating | Steinhart–Hart |
| Respiration | Chest belt / nasal | 0.1 – 10 Hz | Strain / temp | Motion | 12 – 20 bpm normal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generalized Biomedical Measurement Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Biological Signal Source] --> B[Bioelectrode / Sensor]
    B --> C[Input Protection and Isolation]
    C --> D[Instrumentation Amplifier IA]
    D --> E[Bandpass Filter BPF]
    E --> F[Notch Filter 50 Hz Mains Rejection]
    F --> G[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    G --> H[Microcontroller or DSP]
    H --> I[Display LCD or PC]
    H --> J[Actuator Motor or FES]
    J --> K[Patient Feedback Loop]
    K --> A

    subgraph SignalConditioning
        C
        D
        E
        F
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a *stage* in the mechatronic measurement chain. The instrumentation amplifier (IA) is the workhorse — providing high **CMRR (≥ 90 dB)**, high input impedance (> 10 GΩ), and programmable gain (typically 10 – 1000). The notch filter removes **50 Hz (India/Europe)** or **60 Hz (USA)** mains interference.

## 4.2 ECG Lead Configuration — Einthoven's Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RA[RA Right Arm]:::lead
    LA[LA Left Arm]:::lead
    LL[LL Left Leg]:::lead
    RA -- Lead I: LA minus RA --> LA
    LL -- Lead II: LL minus RA --> RA
    LL -- Lead III: LL minus LA --> LA
    classDef lead fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
```

**Lead definitions (for the exam):**
- **Lead I** = potential at LA − potential at RA
- **Lead II** = potential at LL − potential at RA
- **Lead III** = potential at LL − potential at LA

**Einthoven's Law:** $I + III = II$ (Kirchhoff's current law on the triangle).

## 4.3 Pulse Oximeter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Red LED 660 nm] --> B[Photodiode Detector]
    C[IR LED 940 nm] --> B
    B --> D[Transimpedance Amplifier TIA]
    D --> E[Demultiplexer Time Division]
    E --> F[AC and DC Separation]
    F --> G[Ratio of Ratios R Computation]
    G --> H[SpO2 Lookup Table]
    H --> I[Display Digital Readout]

    classDef opt fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#663300
    classDef elec fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px,color:#003300
    class A,C,B opt
    class D,E,F,G,H,I elec
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two LEDs are switched **alternately** (typically at ~1 kHz) so that only one photodiode is required. The "ratio of ratios" $R$ is fed into a pre-calibrated look-up table to yield **SpO₂** (oxygen saturation percentage).

## 4.4 EMG-Driven Prosthetic Limb — Closed-Loop Mechatronic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Residual Muscle EMG] --> B[Surface Electrodes]
    B --> C[Differential Amplifier Gain 1000]
    C --> D[Rectifier and Smoother]
    D --> E[Feature Extraction RMS or MAV]
    E --> F[Classifier SVM or ANN]
    F --> G[Microcontroller]
    G --> H[DC Motor Driver]
    H --> I[Prosthetic Hand]
    I --> J[Force Sensor Feedback]
    J --> G
    I --> K[Patient Visual Feedback]

    classDef bio fill:#e1e1ff,stroke:#3333cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000066
    classDef mecha fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0033,stroke-width:2px,color:#660000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F bio
    class G,H,I,J,K mecha
```

**Working principle (for exam):**
1. **Intent** → user's brain fires motor neurons → muscle generates EMG.
2. EMG captured by **surface electrodes** → amplified → rectified → smoothed → RMS extracted.
3. **Pattern classifier** (machine learning) maps RMS features to one of $N$ grasp classes.
4. **Microcontroller** sends PWM command to motor driver → prosthetic hand closes/opens.
5. **Force/pressure sensor** on the fingertips gives haptic feedback to the controller (closed loop).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define the term "Mean Arterical Pressure" (MAP). Derive its formula in terms of systolic and diastolic blood pressure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) is the average arterial blood pressure over one complete cardiac cycle, weighted according to the time spent in systole and diastole.

**Derivation (2 marks):** The arterial pressure waveform has a duty cycle of approximately 1/3 systole and 2/3 diastole. Hence the time-averaged pressure is:

$$ MAP = \frac{1}{3} SBP + \frac{2}{3} DBP $$

Factoring out DBP gives the **clinical formula**:

$$ MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP) $$

For SBP = 120 mmHg, DBP = 80 mmHg → MAP ≈ **93.33 mmHg**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is Einthoven's triangle? State Einthoven's law and explain its significance in ECG measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Einthoven's Triangle (1.5 marks):** It is the imaginary equilateral triangle formed by placing three electrodes on the **Right Arm (RA)**, **Left Arm (LA)** and **Left Leg (LL)**. The heart is assumed to lie at the centroid. The sides of the triangle represent the three standard bipolar limb leads (I, II, III) used in ECG recording.

**Einthoven's Law (1 mark):** At any instant, the algebraic sum of potentials in Leads I and III equals the potential in Lead II:

$$ I + III = II $$

**Significance (0.5 marks):** It is a consequence of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law; it allows clinicians to verify lead-placement integrity and to derive any one lead from the other two.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice (Pick ONE Option)

### **Option A — 14 Marks**

#### **Question A(a). [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (7 marks)**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the generalized biomedical measurement system. List the role of each block in the chain.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The measurement chain consists of **six sequential blocks**:

1. **Sensor / Bioelectrode (1 mark):** Converts the physiological event (ionic current, pressure, temperature, light) into an electrical signal. Example: Ag/AgCl electrode for ECG, thermistor for temperature, photodiode for SpO₂.

2. **Signal Conditioning (2 marks):** Includes:
   - **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA):** Provides high gain (100 – 10 000), high CMRR (≥ 90 dB), and high input impedance. Typical IC: AD620, INA128.
   - **Band-Pass Filter (BPF):** Removes DC offset and high-frequency noise. ECG: 0.05 – 150 Hz; EMG: 20 – 500 Hz; EEG: 0.5 – 100 Hz.
   - **Notch Filter:** Rejects **50 Hz (India)** mains interference; Q-factor ≥ 30.

3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) (1 mark):** Converts conditioned analog signal to digital. Sampling rate must satisfy **Nyquist** ($f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$); ECG typically sampled at **360 Hz** with **12-bit** resolution.

4. **Microcontroller / DSP (1 mark):** Performs digital filtering, feature extraction (e.g., R-peak detection, RMS for EMG), and decision logic. Examples: ARM Cortex-M4, Texas Instruments MSP430.

5. **Display (0.5 mark):** LCD, OLED, or PC GUI for real-time visualization of waveform or numeric value.

6. **Actuator / Closed-Loop Output (0.5 mark):** In mechatronic applications, the microcontroller drives a motor, FES unit, or alarm. Feedback sensor (e.g., force, position) completes the loop.

**Block diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor] --> B[IA Amplifier] --> C[BPF Filter] --> D[Notch 50 Hz] --> E[ADC] --> F[Microcontroller] --> G[Display]
    F --> H[Actuator]
    H --> I[Feedback Sensor]
    I --> F
```

---

#### **Question A(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (7 marks)**
**Explain the principle and working of a pulse oximeter. Derive the expression for the ratio of ratios R used in SpO₂ computation.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** A pulse oximeter measures arterial oxygen saturation (SpO₂) non-invasively using the **differential absorption** of red (660 nm) and infrared (940 nm) light by oxygenated (HbO₂) and deoxygenated (Hb) haemoglobin in pulsatile arterial blood.

- Hb absorbs more red (660 nm) light.
- HbO₂ absorbs more infrared (940 nm) light.
- Only the **pulsatile (AC) component** of the transmitted light is due to arterial blood; the **DC component** is due to skin, bone, venous blood, etc.

**Working (3 marks):**

1. Two LEDs (660 nm red and 940 nm IR) are placed on one side of a finger clip; a photodiode on the other side detects the transmitted light.
2. The LEDs are switched alternately (time-multiplexed at ~1 kHz).
3. The photodetector current is converted to voltage by a **trans-impedance amplifier (TIA)**.
4. The signal is **demultiplexed** to separate the red and IR channels.
5. The **AC** (pulsatile) and **DC** (baseline) components of each channel are extracted.
6. The **ratio of ratios** $R$ is computed:

$$ R = \frac{(AC_{red} / DC_{red})}{(AC_{IR} / DC_{IR})} $$

7. SpO₂ is obtained from a pre-calibrated empirical look-up table; a widely used linear approximation is:

$$ \text{SpO}_2 \approx 110 - 25 \cdot R \quad [\%] $$

**Derivation of R (2 marks):** From Beer–Lambert law, the absorbance of the arterial blood (pulsatile) component at wavelength $\lambda$ is:

$$ A_{\lambda} = \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{\lambda} \cdot c \cdot d_{AC} $$

where $\varepsilon$ is the molar extinction coefficient, $c$ is concentration, and $d_{AC}$ is the AC path-length change. Taking the ratio of absorbances for red and IR and eliminating the unknown $c \cdot d_{AC}$:

$$ R = \frac{A_{red}}{A_{IR}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{Hb}^{red} - \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{red}}{\varepsilon_{Hb}^{IR} - \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{IR}} \cdot \frac{SaO_2}{1 - SaO_2} $$

The ratio $R$ is therefore a **monotonic function of SpO₂** alone, independent of skin thickness, bone, or venous blood — making it the ideal calibration variable.

---

### **Option B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

#### **Question B(a). [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (7 marks)**
**Explain the measurement of blood pressure using the oscillometric method. Compare it with the auscultatory method.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Oscillometric Method — Working (4 marks):**

1. An inflatable cuff is placed on the upper arm.
2. The cuff is inflated to a pressure above the expected systolic pressure (≈ 180 mmHg), occluding the brachial artery.
3. The cuff is slowly deflated at ≈ 2 – 3 mmHg/s.
4. As pressure drops, the artery begins to pulse, producing small **pressure oscillations** superimposed on the cuff pressure waveform.
5. The amplitude of these oscillations varies as the cuff pressure decreases:
   - At SBP → oscillations first appear.
   - At **MAP** → oscillations reach **maximum amplitude**.
   - As cuff pressure continues to drop → oscillations decrease.
   - At DBP → oscillations abruptly diminish.
6. **Empirical ratio-based algorithms** (e.g., 0.55 × max amplitude for SBP, 0.85 × max amplitude for DBP) extract SBP and DBP from the oscillation envelope.

**Comparison with Auscultatory Method (3 marks):**

| Feature | Auscultatory | Oscillometric |
|---|---|---|
| Detector | Stethoscope + observer | Pressure transducer + algorithm |
| Criterion for SBP/DBP | Korotkoff sounds I and V | Oscillation amplitude envelope |
| Operator skill required | High | Low (automated) |
| Patient movement sensitivity | Moderate | High (artefact rejection needed) |
| Suitable for noisy environment | No | Yes (ICU, ambulance) |
| Gold standard | Yes (clinical reference) | No (slightly less accurate) |
| Patient with atrial fibrillation | May fail | Generally works |

---

#### **Question B(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (7 marks)**
**Describe the components and working of an EMG-driven prosthetic hand. How is the EMG signal processed to control the grip force?**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Components (2 marks):**
- **Surface electrodes** (Ag/AgCl) on the residual forearm muscle.
- **Differential amplifier** (gain 1000, CMRR > 90 dB).
- **Rectifier + low-pass smoother** (envelope detector).
- **Feature extraction unit** (RMS, MAV, Waveform Length).
- **Microcontroller** (e.g., STM32) running a trained **pattern classifier** (SVM, LDA, or simple threshold logic).
- **Motor driver** (H-bridge, e.g., DRV8871).
- **DC motor / servo** coupled to a tendon-driven prosthetic hand.
- **Force/pressure sensor** (FSR) on fingertips for closed-loop control.

**Signal Processing Chain (3 marks):**

1. Raw EMG (50 µV – 5 mV, 20 – 500 Hz) → amplified by IA → bandpass filtered → 50 Hz notch.
2. Full-wave rectified → smoothed by 2nd-order LPF → envelope signal.
3. **RMS** computed over 100 – 200 ms sliding window.
4. RMS value mapped to one of $N$ grip classes (e.g., rest, open, soft close, hard close) using a classifier trained on labelled data.
5. Microcontroller outputs a **PWM duty cycle** proportional to the desired grip force.

**Closed-Loop Force Control (2 marks):**
- A **force-sensitive resistor (FSR)** on each fingertip measures actual grip force.
- The measured force is fed back to the microcontroller.
- A **PID controller** adjusts the PWM duty cycle so that the actual force matches the reference (command from EMG classifier).
- If force exceeds a safety threshold, the motor is reversed to release the grip — preventing crush injury to the grasped object.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Residual Muscle] --> B[EMG Electrodes] --> C[Amplifier] --> D[Rectifier Smoother] --> E[Feature RMS] --> F[Classifier] --> G[PID Controller] --> H[Motor Driver] --> I[Prosthetic Hand]
    I --> J[FSR Force Sensor] --> G
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "ratio of ratios" $R$ with a resistance.** $R$ is a dimensionless optical absorbance ratio.
> 2. **Do NOT state "sampling rate = signal bandwidth."** Sampling rate must be **at least twice** the highest frequency component.
> 3. **In Einthoven's triangle, never mix up Lead II and Lead III.** Lead II = LL − RA; Lead III = LL − LA.
> 4. **In MAP derivation, students often write $MAP = (SBP + DBP)/2$** — this is *wrong*; the correct weighting is **1/3 : 2/3** because diastole lasts longer.
> 5. **In SpO₂, do not write $110 - 25R$ as the definitive formula** — it is an empirical *approximation*; clinical devices use polynomial look-up tables calibrated against co-oximetry.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Physiological parameters are classified as **electrical** (ECG, EMG, EEG), **mechanical** (BP, HR, respiration), **thermal** (temperature), **optical** (SpO₂), and **chemical** (pH, glucose).
- ✅ **Ag/AgCl electrode** is the gold-standard non-polarizable bioelectrode (half-cell potential ≈ **+0.223 V**).
- ✅ **ECG bandwidth = 0.05 – 150 Hz**; EMG = **20 – 500 Hz**; EEG = **0.5 – 100 Hz**.
- ✅ **Nyquist rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. ECG ADC sampling rate in practice = **360 Hz** (or 500 Hz with oversampling).
- ✅ **Einthoven's law:** $I + III = II$ (Kirchhoff's voltage law on the limb triangle).
- ✅ **MAP formula:** $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ — derived from 1/3-systole, 2/3-diastole duty cycle.
- ✅ **Heart rate:** $HR = 60 / RR_{interval}$ in bpm.
- ✅ **Pulse oximetry** uses **660 nm (red) + 940 nm (IR)**; SpO₂ is computed from the **ratio of ratios** $R$.
- ✅ **Oscillometric BP** method: MAP corresponds to the **maximum cuff oscillation amplitude**; SBP and DBP are derived from empirical ratio thresholds (≈ 0.55 and 0.85 of max amplitude).
- ✅ **EMG-driven prosthetics** use the **sensor → IA → rectifier → RMS → classifier → motor** chain, with **FSR-based closed-loop force control**.
- ✅ **Patient safety:** medical instruments must comply with **IEC 60601-1**; maximum patient leakage current = **10 µA (CF)** / **100 µA (BF)**.
- ✅ **Instrumentation amplifier** must have **CMRR ≥ 90 dB** and **input impedance > 10 GΩ** to reject common-mode noise (especially 50 Hz mains).
- ✅ **Poiseuille's law** $Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P r^4}{8 \eta L}$ is the *first-order* teaching approximation for blood flow; real blood is non-Newtonian and vessels are elastic.
- ✅ **EEG bands** to memorize: **δ 0.5–4 Hz**, **θ 4–8 Hz**, **α 8–13 Hz**, **β 13–30 Hz**, **γ > 30 Hz**.
- ✅ Always mention **defibrillator protection (5 kV)**, **galvanic isolation**, and **patient leakage current limits** when designing a medical instrument in a KTU answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrocardiograph Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT864 - BIOMECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Electrocardiograph Measurements ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electrocardiograph (ECG/EKG) Measurement** is the bioelectrical recording technique used to capture the time-varying electrical potential differences generated by the depolarization and repolarization of cardiac muscle cells (myocardium), using surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest. The resulting tracing, the **electrocardiogram**, is a fundamental non-invasive diagnostic tool in clinical cardiology and biomedical engineering. The instrument used to perform this recording is the **electrocardiograph**, and the actual graphical output on paper or screen is the **electrocardiogram (ECG)**. The signal originates from the **sinoatrial (SA) node** — the natural cardiac pacemaker — and propagates through the specialized conduction system: AV node → Bundle of His → bundle branches → Purkinje fibers. **Standard Reference Values (bold constants to memorize):** - **Normal Heart Rate:** 60–100 beats per minute (bpm) - **Normal QRS Duration:** 0.06 – 0.10 s (60–100 ms) - **Normal PR Interval:** 0.12 – 0.20 s (120–200 ms) - **Normal QT Interval:** 0.36 – 0.44 s (corrected for HR) - **ECG Signal Amplitude:** typically **0.5 mV to 5 mV** at the body surface - **Frequency Content:** 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz (diagnostic bandwidth) - **Paper Speed Standard:** **25 mm/s** - **Calibration:** **1 mV = 10 mm vertical deflection** (vertical sensitivity = 10 mm/mV) --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Electrical Symphony of the Heart" Analogy** Imagine the heart as a **3D concert hall** and the cardiac cells as **millions of tiny flashlights** switching on and off in a carefully choreographed sequence. - The **SA node** is the **conductor** who waves the baton first. - The **atrial flashlights** light up together (P wave). - There's a brief **pause in the foyer** (PR segment — signal waits at the AV node). - The **ventricular flashlights** then light up in a massive, rapid wave (QRS complex). - Finally, the **lights dim down** in a relaxed pattern (T wave — repolarization). An ECG machine is like a **microphone placed outside the concert hall** that records the **combined light intensity** leaking through the walls. It cannot see the individual cells, only the **sum of all their electrical activity projected onto the skin's surface**, picked up by electrodes. --- ### 1.3 Why ECG Measurement Matters in Biomechatronics > [!IMPORTANT] > From a **biomechatronics perspective**, ECG measurement is the **input signal backbone** for several closed-loop systems: > - **Pacemakers** (implantable pulse generators — read intrinsic ECG, deliver stimulus) > - **Defibrillators** (Automated External Defibrillators / AEDs — analyze ECG, decide shock) > - **Heart rate monitors** (wearable mechatronic devices — fitness bands, smartwatches) > - **Cardiac rehabilitation robots** (exoskeletons synchronized to cardiac activity) > - **Stress monitoring systems** in HMI (Human-Machine Interface) applications This makes ECG measurement a **core interdisciplinary skill** at the intersection of biology, instrumentation, and signal processing. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Cardiac Electrophysiology — The Origin of the ECG Signal The ECG signal arises from the **action potential** of cardiac myocytes. Unlike skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle has a **long plateau phase (Phase 2)** due to slow Ca²⁺ channel activation — this is why the **QT interval is long** and why the heart cannot be tetanized. **Action Potential Phases (Ventricular myocyte):** - **Phase 0** — Rapid Na⁺ influx → rapid depolarization - **Phase 1** — Transient K⁺ efflux → brief repolarization - **Phase 2** — Plateau (Ca²⁺ influx balances K⁺ efflux) - **Phase 3** — Rapid K⁺ efflux → repolarization - **Phase 4** — Resting membrane potential (~ –90 mV) > [!NOTE] > The **P wave** corresponds to **atrial depolarization** (Phase 0 of atrial cells). The **QRS complex** corresponds to **ventricular depolarization** (Phase 0 of ventricular cells — fast and large). The **T wave** corresponds to **ventricular repolarization** (Phase 3). Atrial repolarization is **hidden inside the QRS complex** because ventricular electrical activity is much larger and masks it. --- ### 2.2 The 12-Lead ECG System The 12-lead ECG is built from **10 electrodes** that produce **12 different "views" (leads)** of the heart's electrical activity. **Two Categories of Leads:** 1. **Bipolar Limb Leads (3 leads):** I, II, III — from **Einthoven's Triangle** 2. **Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (3 leads):** aVR, aVL, aVF 3. **Unipolar Precordial (Chest) Leads (6 leads):** V1, V2, V3, V4, V5, V6 **Einthoven's Triangle:** - **Lead I** = $V_L - V_R$ (Left arm − Right arm) - **Lead II** = $V_F - V_R$ (Left leg − Right arm) - **Lead III** = $V_F - V_L$ (Left leg − Left arm) **Einthoven's Law (key identity):** $$II = I + III$$ This is a **diagnostic consistency check** used in clinical practice. **Augmented Leads** use Wilson's Central Terminal (WCT) as the reference: $$V_{WCT} = \frac{V_L + V_R + V_F}{3}$$ - **aVR** = $V_R - V_{WCT}$ (augmented voltage Right arm) - **aVL** = $V_L - V_{WCT}$ - **aVF** = $V_F - V_{WCT}$ **Precordial Lead Placement (V1–V6):** - **V1** — 4th intercostal space, right sternal border - **V2** — 4th intercostal space, left sternal border - **V3** — midway between V2 and V4 - **V4** — 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line - **V5** — anterior axillary line, same level as V4 - **V6** — mid-axillary line, same level as V4 --- ### 2.3 ECG Waveform Anatomy (High-Yield) | Component | Origin | Duration (s) | Amplitude (mV) | Clinical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | 0.06 – 0.11 | < 0.25 | Atrial enlargement if tall/wide | | **PR Segment** | AV nodal delay | 0.02 – 0.10 | isoelectric | Conduction through AV node | | **PR Interval** | Onset P to onset Q | 0.12 – 0.20 | — | AV block if prolonged | | **QRS Complex** | Ventricular depolarization | 0.06 – 0.10 | 0.5 – 2.5 | Bundle branch block if wide | | **ST Segment** | Ventricular plateau (Phase 2) | 0.05 – 0.15 | isoelectric | Ischemia/Infarction if shifted | | **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 0.10 – 0.25 | < 0.5 | Hyperkalemia if peaked | | **QT Interval** | Total ventricular activity | 0.36 – 0.44 (HR-corrected) | — | Risk of arrhythmia if prolonged | | **U wave** | Late repolarization of Purkinje/papillary muscle | 0.04 – 0.08 | small | Hypokalemia if prominent | --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All high-yield formulas — memorize these for KTU exams.** | # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $II = I + III$ | Einthoven's law | dimensionless (mV) | | 2 | $\text{Heart Rate (bpm)} = \dfrac{1500}{\text{RR interval in small boxes}}$ | Using standard 25 mm/s paper | bpm | | 3 | $\text{Heart Rate (bpm)} = \dfrac{60}{\text{RR interval in seconds}}$ | Direct from RR in seconds | bpm | | 4 | $QT_c = \dfrac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | Bazett's correction formula | seconds | | 5 | $V_{WCT} = \dfrac{V_R + V_L + V_F}{3}$ | Wilson's Central Terminal | mV | | 6 | $\text{Lead I} = V_L - V_R$ | Bipolar limb lead I | mV | | 7 | $\text{Lead II} = V_F - V_R$ | Bipolar limb lead II | mV | | 8 | $\text{Lead III} = V_F - V_L$ | Bipolar limb lead III | mV | | 9 | $\text{Sensitivity} = 10 \text{ mm/mV}$ | Vertical calibration | mm/mV | | 10 | $\text{Paper speed} = 25 \text{ mm/s}$ | Standard ECG paper | mm/s | | 11 | $1 \text{ small box} = 0.04 \text{ s} = 1 \text{ mm}$ | ECG paper small box | s | | 12 | $1 \text{ large box} = 0.20 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ mm}$ | ECG paper large box | s | | 13 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} \right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | dB | | 14 | $G_{total} = G_1 \times G_2 \times \dots \times G_n$ | Multi-stage amplifier gain | dimensionless | | 15 | $f_{low} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | High-pass filter cutoff | Hz | > [!WARNING] > When using the **vertical bar (absolute value)** in formulas within tables in your KTU answer sheets, write $\vert x \vert$ — never use the pipe symbol `|x|`, as it will break Markdown and confuse the examiner. Similarly, use `\vert A_d \vert` style notation. --- ### 2.5 ECG Instrumentation Block Diagram (Conceptual Flow) The signal path is: **Body → Electrodes → Lead Selector → Preamplifier → Power-line Notch Filter → Main Amplifier → Band-pass Filter → A/D Converter → Display/Printer** **Key Design Specifications:** - **Gain required:** ~1000× to bring 1 mV signal to ~1 V level - **CMRR:** > **80 dB** (preferably > 100 dB) to reject 50/60 Hz interference - **Input impedance:** > **10 MΩ** to prevent loading of body - **Bandwidth:** **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** (diagnostic); up to 500 Hz for research - **Notch filter at 50 Hz (India) or 60 Hz (US)** to suppress mains interference ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Heart Rate from RR Interval > [!NOTE] > **Derivation goal:** Establish the two equivalent formulas for heart rate from the RR interval using standard ECG paper. **Step 1 — Recognize the paper speed convention.** At the standard paper speed of **25 mm/s**, in 1 second the pen traces 25 mm of horizontal distance. **Step 2 — Define the small box dimensions.** Since 1 small box = 1 mm horizontally: $$\text{Time per small box} = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm/s}} = 0.04 \text{ s}$$ **Step 3 — Express RR interval in small boxes.** If the RR interval spans **N small boxes**: $$RR = N \times 0.04 \text{ s}$$ **Step 4 — Derive heart rate from RR (in seconds).** By definition, heart rate in bpm = number of beats per 60 seconds. If one RR cycle = RR seconds, then in 60 seconds there are $\frac{60}{RR}$ beats: $$HR = \dfrac{60}{RR} = \dfrac{60}{N \times 0.04}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify the constant.** $$HR = \dfrac{60}{0.04} \times \dfrac{1}{N} = 1500 \times \dfrac{1}{N} = \dfrac{1500}{N}$$ $$\boxed{HR \text{ (bpm)} = \dfrac{1500}{\text{Number of small boxes between two R peaks}}}$$ **Step 6 — Verification.** For N = 20 small boxes (a normal RR): $$HR = \dfrac{1500}{20} = 75 \text{ bpm} \;\checkmark$$ For N = 15 small boxes (fast heart): $$HR = \dfrac{1500}{15} = 100 \text{ bpm} \;\checkmark$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Einthoven's Law ($II = I + III$) **Step 1 — Start with the definitions of the bipolar leads.** Using the lead equations: $$\text{Lead I} = V_L - V_R$$ $$\text{Lead II} = V_F - V_R$$ $$\text{Lead III} = V_F - V_L$$ **Step 2 — Add Lead I and Lead III.** $$I + III = (V_L - V_R) + (V_F - V_L)$$ **Step 3 — Expand and group terms.** $$I + III = V_L - V_R + V_F - V_L$$ **Step 4 — Cancel the $V_L$ terms** (they appear with opposite signs). $$I + III = -V_R + V_F$$ **Step 5 — Recognize the result.** $$I + III = V_F - V_R = \text{Lead II}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \; \text{Lead II} = \text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is **Einthoven's Law**. In practice, it is used to verify the lead connection integrity: if $II \neq I + III$ within ~0.5 mV, there is likely a loose or miswired electrode. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Voltage Calibration Conversion **Step 1 — Standard calibration.** A 1 mV calibration pulse produces a **10 mm vertical deflection**. $$\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{10 \text{ mm}}{1 \text{ mV}} = 10 \text{ mm/mV}$$ **Step 2 — Convert deflection (mm) to voltage (mV).** If a wave has a vertical height of $h$ mm on the ECG: $$V = \dfrac{h}{10} \text{ mV}$$ **Step 3 — Numerical example.** A QRS complex measures 18 mm tall: $$V_{QRS} = \dfrac{18 \text{ mm}}{10 \text{ mm/mV}} = 1.8 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 4 — Convert horizontal distance to time.** At 25 mm/s, a duration of $d$ mm corresponds to: $$t = \dfrac{d}{25} \text{ seconds}$$ **Step 5 — Example: PR interval spans 4 small boxes (4 mm).** $$t_{PR} = \dfrac{4}{25} = 0.16 \text{ s} = 160 \text{ ms}$$ This is within the normal range (120–200 ms). ✓ --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Bandwidth and Filter Cutoff **Step 1 — Need to remove baseline drift (low-frequency noise).** Baseline wander comes from respiration (~0.2 – 0.5 Hz) and electrode DC offset. Use a high-pass filter with cutoff at **0.05 Hz**. **Step 2 — Compute the high-pass filter cutoff.** A first-order RC high-pass filter has: $$f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$$ **Step 3 — Choose a standard capacitor and compute R.** For $C = 1 \mu F$ and $f_c = 0.05$ Hz: $$R = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi (0.05)(1 \times 10^{-6})}$$ $$R = \dfrac{1}{(3.1416)(0.05)(10^{-6})}$$ $$R = \dfrac{1}{1.5708 \times 10^{-7}}$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 6.37 \text{ M}\Omega}$$ **Step 4 — Need to remove high-frequency muscle (EMG) noise.** Use a low-pass filter with cutoff at **150 Hz** (diagnostic standard). **Step 5 — For $C = 100$ nF and $f_c = 150$ Hz:** $$R = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi (150)(100 \times 10^{-9})}$$ $$R = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi (1.5 \times 10^{-5})}$$ $$R = \dfrac{1}{9.4248 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 10.61 \text{ k}\Omega}$$ > [!NOTE] > A practical ECG machine uses a **band-pass filter from 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**, realized using op-amp active filter topologies (Sallen-Key, multiple-feedback) for sharper roll-off than passive RC. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common ECG Abnormalities vs. Waveform Changes | Condition | Waveform Change | Clinical Interpretation | |---|---|---| | Atrial Enlargement | P wave > 0.25 mV (P mitrale / P pulmonale) | Atrial hypertrophy | | First-Degree AV Block | PR interval > 0.20 s | Delayed AV conduction | | Bundle Branch Block | QRS > 0.12 s, wide and bizarre | Block in His-Purkinje system | | Myocardial Ischemia | ST segment depression (≥ 0.5 mm) | Inadequate blood flow | | Myocardial Infarction (STEMI) | ST segment elevation (≥ 1 mm) | Acute coronary occlusion | | Hyperkalemia | Tall, peaked T waves | Excess potassium | | Hypokalemia | Prominent U waves, flattened T | Potassium deficiency | | Atrial Fibrillation | Irregularly irregular, no P waves | Chaotic atrial activity | | Ventricular Fibrillation | Chaotic, no discernible QRS | Medical emergency | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 ECG Waveform Architecture (P-QRS-T-U Sequence) The following Mermaid diagram maps the **chronological sequence and electrical events** of the ECG waveform. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["SA Node Fires"] --> nodeB["P Wave Begins
Atrial Depolarization"] nodeB --> nodeC["P Wave Peak
0.25 mV max"] nodeC --> nodeD["PR Segment
AV Node Delay"] nodeD --> nodeE["PR Interval Ends
0.12 to 0.20 s"] nodeE --> nodeF["Q Wave Starts
Septal Depolarization"] nodeF --> nodeG["R Wave Peak
Ventricular Mass Depolarization"] nodeG --> nodeH["S Wave Downstroke"] nodeH --> nodeI["QRS Complete
0.06 to 0.10 s"] nodeI --> nodeJ["ST Segment
Plateau Phase 2"] nodeJ --> nodeK["T Wave
Ventricular Repolarization"] nodeK --> nodeL["U Wave Optional
Papillary Muscle"] nodeL --> nodeM["Cycle Repeats"] ``` --- ### 4.2 12-Lead ECG System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["10 Surface Electrodes
4 Limb + 6 Precordial"] --> nodeB["Lead Selector
Switch Matrix"] nodeB --> nodeC1["Bipolar Leads
I II III"] nodeB --> nodeC2["Augmented Leads
aVR aVL aVF"] nodeB --> nodeC3["Precordial Leads
V1 V2 V3 V4 V5 V6"] nodeC1 --> nodeD["Preamplifier
Gain 10x
CMRR 100 dB"] nodeC2 --> nodeD nodeC3 --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE["Notch Filter
50 Hz Rejection"] nodeE --> nodeF["Main Amplifier
Gain 100x"] nodeF --> nodeG["Bandpass Filter
0.05 to 150 Hz"] nodeG --> nodeH["ADC
12 to 16 bit"] nodeH --> nodeI["Microcontroller
DSP Processing"] nodeI --> nodeJ["Display / Printer
LCD / Thermal"] ``` --- ### 4.3 Einthoven's Triangle and Limb Lead Vectors ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeR["R
Right Arm"] --- nodeL["L
Left Arm"] nodeL --- nodeF["F
Left Leg"] nodeF --- nodeR nodeR -->|Lead I Vector| nodeL nodeL -->|Lead III Vector| nodeF nodeR -->|Lead II Vector| nodeF nodeR -.- nodeAVR["aVR
augmented"] nodeL -.- nodeAVL["aVL
augmented"] nodeF -.- nodeAVF["aVF
augmented"] ``` --- ### 4.4 ECG Front-End Amplifier Signal Path ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeInput["Body Surface Potential
0.5 to 5 mV"] --> nodeE["Electrode
Ag/AgCl"] nodeE --> nodeIC["Instrumentation
Amplifier INA128"] nodeIC --> nodeHPF["HPF at 0.05 Hz
Drift Removal"] nodeHPF --> nodeGain["Variable Gain
100 to 5000x"] nodeGain --> nodeNotch["50 Hz Notch
Twin-T Filter"] nodeNotch --> nodeLPF["LPF at 150 Hz
EMG Removal"] nodeLPF --> nodeBuffer["Unity Buffer
Output Driver"] nodeBuffer --> nodeOut["Output to ADC
0 to 5 V range"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., INA128, AD620) is the standard front-end for ECG. It provides high CMRR (>100 dB), high input impedance (>10 GΩ), and adjustable gain using a single external resistor. --- ### 4.5 Electrode Types Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["ECG Electrodes"] --> nodeB["Surface Electrodes"] nodeA --> nodeC["Invasive Electrodes"] nodeB --> nodeD["Ag/AgCl
Disposable Gel"] nodeB --> nodeE["Suction Cup
Reusable"] nodeB --> nodeF["Dry Contact
Wearable"] nodeC --> nodeG["Intracardiac
Catheter"] nodeC --> nodeH["Esophageal
Lead"] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the term "Electrocardiogram" and list any four characteristics of a normal ECG waveform.** **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** The **electrocardiogram (ECG)** is the graphical recording of the electrical activity of the heart, obtained by placing surface electrodes on the body and amplifying the potential differences between them. **Four characteristics of a normal ECG:** 1. **P wave:** small, rounded, upright in Lead II; duration 0.06 – 0.11 s, amplitude < 0.25 mV — represents atrial depolarization. 2. **QRS complex:** sharp, tall deflection; duration 0.06 – 0.10 s, amplitude 0.5 – 2.5 mV — represents ventricular depolarization. 3. **T wave:** smooth, asymmetric, upright in Lead II; amplitude < 0.5 mV — represents ventricular repolarization. 4. **PR interval:** 0.12 – 0.20 s, measured from onset of P to onset of QRS — represents the time taken for atrial depolarization + AV nodal delay. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **What is Einthoven's Triangle? State Einthoven's Law with its significance.** **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** **Einthoven's Triangle** is an imaginary equilateral triangle formed around the heart, with its vertices at the three limb electrodes: **Right Arm (R)**, **Left Arm (L)**, and **Left Leg (F)**. The cardiac electrical vector is assumed to lie in the center of this triangle. **Einthoven's Law:** $$II = I + III$$ i.e., the voltage recorded in **Lead II equals the algebraic sum of Lead I and Lead III** when measured simultaneously. **Significance:** - Verifies **proper electrode connection** during ECG recording. - If the law fails by more than ~0.5 mV, it indicates **loose contact, miswired cables, or electrode polarization artifacts**. - It forms the theoretical basis for the **augmented unipolar leads** (aVR, aVL, aVF). --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** **a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an ECG machine. Describe each block in detail. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Block Diagram:** ``` Body → Electrodes → Lead Selector → Instrumentation Amplifier → Notch Filter (50 Hz) → Main Amplifier → Band-pass Filter → ADC → Microcontroller → Display/Printer ``` **Block-by-Block Explanation:** 1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** Convert ionic currents in the body into electronic currents; placed on limbs and chest at 10 standard positions. 2. **Lead Selector Switch:** A multiplexer that selects any pair (or combination) of electrodes to feed the amplifier. Allows switching between 12 standard views (leads I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, V1–V6). 3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (e.g., INA128):** The first-stage amplifier with: - High **CMRR > 100 dB** to reject common-mode noise (e.g., 50 Hz mains). - High **input impedance > 10 MΩ** to avoid body loading. - Gain typically set to **10×** to bring 1 mV → 10 mV. 4. **Notch Filter (50 Hz Twin-T):** Sharply rejects 50 Hz power-line interference while preserving ECG frequencies (0.05–150 Hz) on either side. 5. **Main Amplifier:** Provides additional gain (100×–1000×) to bring signal to ~1 V level for ADC. 6. **Band-pass Filter (0.05 Hz HPF + 150 Hz LPF):** Removes baseline wander (low-frequency respiration artifacts) and high-frequency EMG/muscle noise. 7. **ADC (12–16 bit, sampling ≥ 500 Hz):** Converts the analog ECG into digital samples for digital processing. 8. **Microcontroller / DSP:** Runs digital filters, detects R-peaks, computes heart rate, detects arrhythmias, and drives the display. 9. **Display / Thermal Printer:** Shows the waveform on LCD or prints on thermal paper at 25 mm/s with 10 mm/mV calibration. --- **b) A patient has an ECG recording where the RR interval measures 24 small boxes on standard paper. Calculate the heart rate. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Recall paper speed convention.** At 25 mm/s with small box = 1 mm, each small box = 0.04 s. **Step 2 — Convert RR interval to time.** $$RR = 24 \times 0.04 = 0.96 \text{ s}$$ **Step 3 — Apply heart rate formula.** $$HR = \dfrac{60}{RR} = \dfrac{60}{0.96}$$ $$\boxed{HR = 62.5 \text{ bpm}}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** A heart rate of 62.5 bpm lies within the **normal resting range of 60–100 bpm**. Therefore, this patient has **normal sinus rhythm** with no bradycardia or tachycardia. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]** **a) Explain the 12-lead ECG system. Describe the placement of any 4 precordial leads with their anatomical landmarks. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The **12-lead ECG** is a system of 10 surface electrodes that produces **12 different electrical "views"** of the heart's activity: **A. Three Bipolar Limb Leads (from Einthoven's Triangle):** - **Lead I** = $V_L - V_R$ - **Lead II** = $V_F - V_R$ - **Lead III** = $V_F - V_L$ **B. Three Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (using Wilson's Central Terminal as reference):** - **aVR, aVL, aVF** **C. Six Unipolar Precordial (Chest) Leads (V1–V6):** | Lead | Anatomical Landmark | |---|---| | **V1** | 4th intercostal space, right sternal border | | **V2** | 4th intercostal space, left sternal border | | **V3** | Midway between V2 and V4 | | **V4** | 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line | | **V5** | Anterior axillary line, same horizontal level as V4 | | **V6** | Mid-axillary line, same horizontal level as V4 | **Anatomical Note:** V1 and V2 lie over the **right ventricle**, V3 and V4 over the **interventricular septum and left ventricle (anterior wall)**, and V5 and V6 over the **left ventricle (lateral wall)**. This makes them diagnostic for **anterior vs. lateral myocardial infarctions**. --- **b) An ECG machine has an overall gain of 1000. The first-stage (instrumentation) amplifier has a gain of 10 and the second-stage amplifier has a gain of 50. If a third stage with a gain of 2 is added, what is the new overall gain? Also, what input voltage produces a 1 V output? (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Original overall gain verification.** $$G_{total} = G_1 \times G_2 = 10 \times 50 = 500$$ (Note: original 1000 may be a typo or includes a third stage — we proceed with the new addition.) **Step 2 — New overall gain with third stage.** $$G_{new} = G_1 \times G_2 \times G_3 = 10 \times 50 \times 2$$ $$\boxed{G_{new} = 1000}$$ **Step 3 — Required input for 1 V output.** By definition, $V_{out} = G \times V_{in}$: $$V_{in} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{G_{new}} = \dfrac{1 \text{ V}}{1000}$$ $$V_{in} = 0.001 \text{ V} = 1 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** This 1 mV input is exactly the typical amplitude of ECG signals at the skin. The 1000× total gain brings it to a 1 V level — ideal for ADC input (typically 0–5 V range). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):** > > 1. **Forgetting to state the lead equation definition** when asked about Einthoven's law — you MUST write $\text{Lead II} = V_F - V_R$ before adding. > 2. **Mixing up "lead" vs. "electrode"** — students often confuse these. A *lead* is a *view* (a voltage difference); an *electrode* is the physical sensor. > 3. **Not labeling axis values on diagrams** — always show $\text{mV}$ on Y-axis and $\text{seconds}$ on X-axis in ECG waveforms. > 4. **Skipping units in final answers** — KTU strictly penalizes missing units (write `bpm`, `mV`, `s`). > 5. **Wrong paper speed** — must remember 25 mm/s, NOT 50 mm/s. > 6. **Misplacing V1 and V2** — V1 is on the **right** side of the sternum, V2 is on the **left**. > 7. **In heart rate problems**, always state the formula before substituting — partial credit depends on showing the method. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — ECG Measurements** - ✅ ECG measures **potential differences** at the body surface; the signal originates from **cardiac action potentials**. - ✅ Normal amplitude at skin: **0.5 to 5 mV** → requires ~**1000× gain**. - ✅ Standard **paper speed = 25 mm/s**; **sensitivity = 10 mm/mV**. - ✅ 1 small box = **1 mm = 0.04 s = 40 ms**; 1 large box = **5 mm = 0.20 s**. - ✅ Heart rate formula: $HR = 1500 / \text{small boxes between R peaks}$. - ✅ Normal sinus rhythm HR: **60–100 bpm**. - ✅ **Einthoven's Triangle** vertices: **R, L, F** (Right arm, Left arm, Left leg). - ✅ **Einthoven's Law:** $II = I + III$ — always verify electrode connections. - ✅ Lead I = $V_L - V_R$; Lead II = $V_F - V_R$; Lead III = $V_F - V_L$. - ✅ **P wave** = atrial depolarization; **QRS** = ventricular depolarization; **T wave** = ventricular repolarization. - ✅ **PR interval** (0.12–0.20 s) reflects AV conduction; **QT interval** (corrected for HR) reflects total ventricular activity. - ✅ **12-lead ECG** = 3 bipolar + 3 augmented + 6 precordial = 12 views from 10 electrodes. - ✅ **Precordial V1** at 4th intercostal space, right sternal border; **V4** at 5th intercostal, mid-clavicular line. - ✅ Instrumentation amplifier specs: **CMRR > 100 dB**, **input Z > 10 MΩ**. - ✅ Diagnostic bandwidth: **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**; **50 Hz notch** for Indian mains. - ✅ Three common ECG artifacts: **baseline wander (low-freq)**, **50/60 Hz interference (notch)**, **muscle noise (EMG, high-freq)**. - ✅ Ag/AgCl electrodes are **non-polarizable** — preferred for accurate ECG. - ✅ Atrial repolarization is **hidden inside the QRS** (masked by larger ventricular signal). - ✅ **QTc (Bazett's)** = $QT / \sqrt{RR}$ — corrected for heart rate. - ✅ The ECG is the **input signal** for closed-loop mechatronic systems: **pacemakers, AEDs, cardiac rehab exoskeletons**.

topics.title: Blood Pressure Measurement by Ultrasonic Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT864 - BIOMECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Blood Pressure Measurement by Ultrasonic Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Blood Pressure (BP):** The lateral force exerted by the column of blood on the walls of the arteries, expressed as the systolic (maximum, during ventricular contraction) and diastolic (minimum, during ventricular relaxation) pressures. Standard clinical unit: **mmHg** (millimeters of mercury); SI equivalent: **kPa** (1 mmHg ≈ 0.133 kPa).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultrasonic Method of BP Measurement:** A non-invasive, indirect technique that utilizes high-frequency mechanical sound waves (typically **2 MHz to 10 MHz**) directed at a peripheral artery to detect arterial wall motion and blood flow dynamics. It is based on either the **Doppler shift principle** or the **transit-time (time-of-flight) principle** to identify the Korotkoff sound equivalents and arterial pulsations, thereby determining systolic and diastolic blood pressure without arterial puncture.

| Parameter | Typical Value (Adult) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Systolic BP (SBP) | **120** | mmHg |
| Diastolic BP (DBP) | **80** | mmHg |
| Pulse Pressure (PP) | **40** | mmHg |
| Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) | **93** | mmHg |
| Nominal Heart Rate (NHR) | **60–100** | beats/min |
| Ultrasonic Frequency (f₀) | **2–10** | MHz |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Subway-Tunnel Doppler" Intuition

Imagine standing at the mouth of a tunnel as a subway train approaches. As the train comes **toward** you, the pitch of its horn sounds *higher* than its true value. As it **moves away**, the pitch drops *lower*. The same phenomenon happens with ultrasound waves bouncing off moving red blood cells inside an artery.

- The **train** = red blood cells flowing in the brachial artery.
- The **horn** = the transmitted ultrasonic wave.
- **Your ear** = the piezoelectric receiver.
- **The pitch change** = the **Doppler frequency shift (Δf)**.

When an external cuff pressure equals systolic pressure, the artery just begins to open, blood spurts through, and Δf jumps. When cuff pressure equals diastolic pressure, flow becomes laminar and continuous, and Δf returns to baseline. By reading the cuff pressure at these two instants, we determine SBP and DBP.

> [!TIP]
> **Why ultrasound?** Unlike a stethoscope, ultrasound is **insensitive to ambient noise**, works through layers of fat (useful in obese patients), can be used on neonates, and is capable of **continuous** monitoring in critical care — a key advantage in mechatronic patient-monitoring systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Principles Underlying the Method

The ultrasonic blood-pressure monitor is a closed-loop mechatronic system comprising **sensing, signal-conditioning, decision, and display** subsystems. Three physical laws govern its operation:

1. **Piezoelectric Effect (Transduction):** Certain crystals (lead zirconate titanate — PZT, or polyvinylidene fluoride — PVDF) convert an applied AC voltage into mechanical vibration (transmitter mode), and conversely convert received mechanical vibration into an electrical signal (receiver mode). A single crystal commonly does both jobs.
2. **Doppler Effect (Flow Detection):** A wave reflected from a moving target exhibits a frequency shift proportional to the target's velocity component along the wave's axis of propagation.
3. **Acoustic Impedance Mismatch (Reflection):** The acoustic impedance $Z = \rho c$ (where $\rho$ = tissue density, $c$ = speed of sound) differs between blood, vessel wall, and surrounding tissue. The reflection coefficient at each interface produces detectable echoes.

## 2.2 Operational Workflow (Step-Wise Logic)

- **Step 1 — Cuff Inflation:** A pneumatic cuff is wrapped around the upper arm and inflated by a pump (often a DC motor-driven diaphragm pump) to a pressure above the expected SBP (typically 160–200 mmHg). This occludes the brachial artery completely.
- **Step 2 — Piezo Excitation:** An oscillator drives a piezo crystal at a nominal frequency $f_0$ (commonly **2 MHz** or **5 MHz**). The crystal emits a continuous-wave (CW) or pulsed ultrasonic beam.
- **Step 3 — Beam-Tissue Coupling:** A water-based or gel couplant eliminates the air gap between the transducer and skin (acoustic impedance of air is ~$430$ rayl, that of skin is ~$1.6 \times 10^6$ rayl — a mismatch of ~4000×, hence the need for gel).
- **Step 4 — Wave Propagation & Reflection:** The beam penetrates tissue and reflects off three principal interfaces: (a) the anterior arterial wall, (b) the moving red blood cell column, (c) the posterior arterial wall.
- **Step 5 — Doppler Detection:** Reflected waves from the moving RBCs return with a shifted frequency $f_0 \pm \Delta f$. The stationary wall reflections return at $f_0$ unchanged.
- **Step 6 — Controlled Deflation:** A solenoid valve slowly releases cuff pressure at ~2–3 mmHg/s. A pressure transducer (strain-gauge or piezoresistive) tracks cuff pressure in real time.
- **Step 7 — Event Detection:** As cuff pressure crosses SBP, the artery snaps open, blood spurts through, and a sudden Doppler "whoosh" is detected. As cuff pressure crosses DBP, the artery remains fully open, flow becomes laminar, and the Doppler signal becomes silent or smooth.
- **Step 8 — Display & Logging:** A microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M class) timestamps the SBP and DBP events, computes MAP and pulse rate, and drives an LCD/touchscreen display or transmits the data via Bluetooth/UART.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these six equations — they constitute > 90% of the numerical problems examiners set on this topic.

$$
f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}
$$

$$
v = \frac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

$$
c_{tissue} = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}
$$

$$
Z = \rho \, c
$$

$$
MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)
$$

**Symbol Table (Examiner-Ready):**

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $f_0$ | Transmitted (carrier) ultrasonic frequency | MHz |
| $f_d$ | Doppler frequency shift | Hz |
| $v$ | Blood flow velocity | m/s |
| $\theta$ | Beam-vessel angle (angle of insonation) | degrees |
| $c$ | Speed of ultrasound in soft tissue | m/s |
| $\rho$ | Density of the propagation medium | kg/m³ |
| $K$ | Bulk modulus of the medium | Pa |
| $Z$ | Acoustic impedance | rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) |
| $R$ | Pressure reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| $MAP$ | Mean arterial pressure | mmHg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** $1$ MHz $= 10^6$ Hz. The Doppler shift $f_d$ for a 5 MHz beam and typical arterial flow ($v \approx 0.5$ m/s) at $\theta = 45°$ is on the order of a few hundred Hz — well within audible range after demodulation. This is why the output of an ultrasonic BP monitor can be listened to with headphones, mimicking the auscultatory method.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Patient monitors** in ICUs, CCUs, and OTs (e.g., Philips IntelliVue, GE CARESCAPE).
- **Ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM)** for 24-hour hypertension profiling.
- **Fetal heart-rate detectors** during pregnancy (handheld Doppler units).
- **Vascular diagnostics** — stenosis, deep vein thrombosis, valve incompetence.
- **Sports & wearable biomedical devices** — wrist-worn cuffless BP estimators using PPG + ECG + ultrasonic sensors.
- **Closed-loop anesthesia systems** that titrate drugs based on real-time MAP.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Doppler Shift Equation $f_d = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$

We consider a piezo transmitter at rest, a red blood cell (RBC) moving with velocity $v$ along the artery axis, and a stationary receiver (in practice, the same crystal acts as both, but we analyze the two-way trip).

**Step 1 — Frequency observed by the moving RBC (transmitter → moving target).**

The RBC acts as a moving observer. The frequency $f_1$ it "hears" is given by the classical Doppler formula for a stationary source and a moving observer:

$$
f_1 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right)
$$

(Positive sign: the observer moves *toward* the source.)

**Step 2 — Frequency of the wave re-emitted (scattered) by the RBC.**

The RBC re-radiates (scatters) the wave at the same frequency it perceives, $f_1$. It is now a *moving source* of frequency $f_1$.

**Step 3 — Frequency detected by the stationary receiver (moving source → stationary observer).**

The stationary piezo receives the re-radiated wave. For a moving source approaching a stationary observer:

$$
f_2 = f_1 \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)
$$

(Negative sign in denominator: source moves *toward* the observer.)

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 3.**

$$
f_2 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right) \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)
$$

$$
f_2 = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the engineering approximation $v \cos\theta \ll c$.**

Blood velocity ($v \approx 0.1$ to 1 m/s) is orders of magnitude smaller than the speed of sound in tissue ($c \approx 1540$ m/s). Hence $v \cos\theta / c \ll 1$.

Using the binomial expansion $\dfrac{1 + x}{1 - x} \approx 1 + 2x$ for $x \ll 1$ with $x = \dfrac{v \cos\theta}{c}$:

$$
f_2 \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Define the Doppler shift.**

$$
f_d = f_2 - f_0
$$

$$
\boxed{f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the blood velocity (the inverse problem, useful clinically).**

$$
\boxed{v = \frac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor of **2** appears because the wave undergoes a **two-way** trip (transmitted, then reflected). If the receiver were a separate stationary probe and the RBC a moving source only, the formula would have a factor of **1**, not 2.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Calibrated)

**Problem:** A 5 MHz ultrasonic beam is directed at the brachial artery at an angle $\theta = 60°$. The Doppler shift measured is $f_d = 440$ Hz. The speed of ultrasound in soft tissue is $c = 1540$ m/s. Find the peak blood flow velocity.

**Solution:**

$$
v = \frac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

$$
v = \frac{440 \times 1540}{2 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times \cos 60°}
$$

$$
v = \frac{440 \times 1540}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5}
$$

$$
v = \frac{677{,}600}{5 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
v = 0.1355 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v \approx 13.55 \text{ cm/s}
$$

**Verification:** This is a reasonable value — peak systolic velocity in a healthy brachial artery typically lies between **10 cm/s and 100 cm/s**. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Error & Parameter Sensitivity

| Source of Inaccuracy | Effect on Computed $v$ | Mitigation in Mechatronic Design |
|---|---|---|
| Misalignment of $\theta$ (cosine error) | $v \propto 1/\cos\theta$ — large for $\theta \to 90°$ | Mechanical fixture / dual-beam geometry / angle-compensation algorithm |
| Tissue inhomogeneity changing $c$ | $v \propto c$ — small but cumulative | Use tabulated $c$ for the dominant tissue; temperature compensation |
| Motion artifact (probe moves) | Spurious $f_d$ from probe velocity | Accelerometer-based motion cancellation |
| Cuff-leak rate too fast | SBP underestimated, DBP overestimated | Servo-controlled solenoid valve; flow-restrictor orifice |
| PZT crystal aging | Reduced sensitivity, $S/N$ drops | Periodic self-test routine in firmware |
| Air gap at skin interface | >99% of beam reflected — no signal | Mandatory water-based couplant gel |

## 3.4 Mean Arterial Pressure Derivation

The MAP is the time-averaged pressure over one cardiac cycle. Approximating the arterial pressure waveform as a constant diastolic plateau of duration $t_d$ followed by a roughly triangular systolic peak of base $t_s$ (with $t_d + t_s = T$ the cardiac period):

$$
MAP = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T P(t) \, dt = \frac{P_d \, t_d + \tfrac{1}{2}(P_s + P_d) \, t_s}{T}
$$

Assuming a typical duty ratio $t_s / T \approx 1/3$ (systole occupies one-third of the cycle):

$$
MAP = \frac{P_d \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} T + \tfrac{1}{2}(P_s + P_d) \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} T}{T} = \tfrac{2}{3} P_d + \tfrac{1}{6}(P_s + P_d) = P_d + \tfrac{1}{3}(P_s - P_d)
$$

$$
\boxed{MAP = DBP + \tfrac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram — Ultrasonic BP Monitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ARM Cortex M4 MCU Controller] --> B[Oscillator 2 to 10 MHz]
    B --> C[Piezo Transducer PZT]
    C -->|US beam into tissue| D[Brachial Artery]
    D -->|Reflected shifted echo| C
    C --> E[RF Amplifier 40 dB]
    E --> F[Phase Shift Demodulator]
    F --> G[Audio Amplifier 100 Hz to 5 kHz]
    G --> H1[Speaker Headphones]
    G --> H2[Comparator and Event Detector]
    H2 --> A
    A --> I[Pump Motor Driver]
    I --> J[Diaphragm Pump]
    J --> K[Pneumatic Cuff]
    K --> L[Pressure Transducer Strain Gauge]
    L --> M[ADC 12 bit]
    M --> A
    A --> N[LCD Touch Display]
    A --> O[Bluetooth UART to Phone]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **forward path** (left-to-right top branch) is the **ultrasonic transmit-receive** chain. The **bottom path** is the **cuff-pressure regulation** chain. The **MCU** closes the loop, fusing Doppler events with cuff-pressure data to compute SBP/DBP/MAP.

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Cuff-Deflation vs Doppler-Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Power On]) --> init[Initialize MCU and Calibrate Sensors]
    init --> inflate[Pump Air Into Cuff Above SBP Threshold 180 mmHg]
    inflate --> startUS[Activate US Transducer at 5 MHz CW]
    startUS --> acquire[Acquire Reflected Echo Continuously]
    acquire --> check{Is Doppler Burst Detected}
    check -- No --> deflate[Solenoid Valve Bleeds Air at 2 to 3 mmHg per second]
    deflate --> pcuff[Read Live Cuff Pressure P_c]
    pcuff --> check
    check -- Yes --> markSBP[Record P_c as SBP and Timestamp]
    markSBP --> cont[Continue Deflation and Monitoring]
    cont --> check2{Has Doppler Signal Disappeared}
    check2 -- No --> cont
    check2 -- Yes --> markDBP[Record P_c as DBP and Timestamp]
    markDBP --> vent[Vent Cuff Fully to Zero Pressure]
    vent --> compute[Compute MAP and Pulse Rate]
    compute --> display[Display Results on LCD and Transmit via BLE]
    display --> done([Cycle Complete and Standby])
```

## 4.3 Transducer-to-Artery Geometric Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph skin[Transducer On Skin Surface]
        T[PZT Crystal 5 MHz]
    end
    subgraph art[Artery Cross Section]
        AW1[Anterior Wall]
        RBC[Red Blood Cell Column Moving at v]
        AW2[Posterior Wall]
    end
    T -->|Incident US f0| AW1
    AW1 -->|Wall echo unchanged f0| T
    T -->|Penetrates to RBCs| RBC
    RBC -->|Doppler shifted echo f0 plus fd| T
    T -->|Penetrates further| AW2
    AW2 -->|Posterior wall echo| T
```

> [!NOTE]
> The angle $\theta$ in the Doppler equation is the angle between the **ultrasonic beam axis** and the **artery's longitudinal axis** — *not* the angle between the beam and the skin. Proper coupling geometry maximizes the projected velocity component along the beam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the Doppler effect and state the Doppler shift equation used in ultrasonic blood-flow measurement. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Doppler effect** is the apparent change in frequency of a wave observed whenever there is relative motion between the source of the wave and the observer. In ultrasonic blood-flow measurement, a piezo transducer transmits a wave of frequency $f_0$ into tissue; the wave reflects from moving red blood cells and returns with a shifted frequency $f_0 + f_d$.

The Doppler shift for a two-way trip (transmit + reflect) is given by:

$$
f_d = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}
$$

where $v$ is the blood-flow velocity, $\theta$ the angle of insonation, and $c$ the speed of ultrasound in tissue (~1540 m/s). **(3 marks — definition 1, context 1, equation 1.)**

---

### Q2. List any three advantages of the ultrasonic method of blood-pressure measurement over the auscultatory method. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Insensitive to ambient noise** — useful in ICUs and ambulances where stethoscope auscultation is impossible. (1 mark)
2. **Objective and automatable** — the Doppler shift is a quantitative electronic signal; results do not depend on the examiner's hearing acuity. (1 mark)
3. **Usable on neonates, obese patients, and in low-blood-pressure states** where Korotkoff sounds are weak or absent; the ultrasonic beam directly senses arterial wall motion. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write!**
> - **Never** write the Doppler equation as $f_d = (f_0 v \cos\theta)/c$ — the factor of **2** is mandatory for a two-way trip. Lose 1 mark if you forget it.
> - **Never** confuse the angle of insonation $\theta$ with the angle the beam makes with the **skin** surface. $\theta$ is measured from the **artery axis**.
> - **Always** state the speed of sound in soft tissue as $c = 1540$ m/s unless the problem specifies otherwise.
> - For MAP derivations, show the **integration** or the duty-ratio assumption explicitly; do not write the final formula as a memorized result.
> - Mention the **couplant gel** and the **piezoelectric transducer** in any block-diagram question — they are favorite 1-mark fillers.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **ultrasonic blood-pressure monitor** using the Doppler principle. Label all functional blocks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Doppler shift equation** $f_d = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ starting from the basic observer-source Doppler relations. State clearly the approximation used and justify it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

1. **Block Diagram** (drawn as in Section 4.1 above) — **2 marks** for the diagram; **1 mark** for correct labeling of all functional blocks (oscillator, transducer, RF amplifier, demodulator, cuff, pressure transducer, ADC, MCU, display).
2. **Working principle** (3 × 1 mark):
   - A CW ultrasonic beam of frequency $f_0$ is directed at the brachial artery via a piezo transducer with gel couplant. (1 mark)
   - The reflected signal is Doppler-shifted by an amount $f_d$ proportional to RBC velocity. (1 mark)
   - As cuff pressure falls, arterial opening events generate Doppler bursts — the cuff pressure at the first burst = SBP; the pressure at the last burst = DBP. (1 mark)
3. **Functional role of the MCU** (1 mark): time-stamps events, computes MAP, drives the display, and closes the cuff-pressure control loop.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

(See Section 3.1 above for the full derivation. The examiner awards marks as follows:)

- **Step 1** — Frequency observed by moving RBC: $f_1 = f_0 \left( \dfrac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right)$ — **1 mark**
- **Step 2** — Frequency of scattered wave: $f_1$ re-radiated — **1 mark**
- **Step 3** — Frequency detected by stationary receiver: $f_2 = f_1 \left( \dfrac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$ — **1 mark**
- **Step 4** — Substitution and simplification — **1 mark**
- **Step 5** — Binomial approximation: $\dfrac{1+x}{1-x} \approx 1 + 2x$ for $x \ll 1$, with $x = \dfrac{v \cos\theta}{c}$ — **1 mark**
- **Step 6** — Justification of the approximation: $v \sim 0.5$ m/s, $c \sim 1540$ m/s, ratio $\sim 3 \times 10^{-4} \ll 1$ — **1 mark**
- **Step 7** — Final boxed equation $f_d = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ — **1 mark**

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** An ultrasonic blood-flow meter uses a **5 MHz** transducer. The angle between the beam and the artery is **45°**, and the measured Doppler shift is **600 Hz**. If the speed of ultrasound in soft tissue is **1540 m/s**, calculate the **blood-flow velocity**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **transit-time (time-of-flight) ultrasonic method** of blood-flow measurement. How does it differ from the Doppler method? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

Given: $f_0 = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz, $\theta = 45°$, $f_d = 600$ Hz, $c = 1540$ m/s.

**Step 1** — State the Doppler-shift equation: $f_d = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ — **1 mark**

**Step 2** — Solve for $v$: $v = \dfrac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ — **1 mark**

**Step 3** — Substitute:

$$
v = \frac{600 \times 1540}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times \cos 45°}
$$

— **1 mark**

**Step 4** — Evaluate $\cos 45° = 0.7071$ and denominator: $2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.7071 = 7.071 \times 10^6$ — **1 mark**

**Step 5** — Numerator: $600 \times 1540 = 924{,}000$ — **1 mark**

**Step 6** — Final division:

$$
v = \frac{924{,}000}{7.071 \times 10^6} = 0.1306 \text{ m/s} \approx 13.06 \text{ cm/s}
$$

— **1 mark**

**Step 7** — Verification comment: this is physiologically reasonable for a brachial-artery peak systolic velocity. — **1 mark**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

- **Principle** (2 marks): The transit-time method uses two piezo transducers placed on opposite sides of the vessel (an "upstream" and a "downstream" probe). Each emits an ultrasonic pulse; the pulse traveling **with** the flow arrives sooner, and the pulse traveling **against** the flow arrives later. The **time difference** $\Delta t$ between the two arrivals is proportional to the average flow velocity:

$$
v = \frac{c^2 \, \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta}
$$

where $L$ is the path length between the probes.

- **Distinguishing features** (5 × 1 mark each):
   1. Doppler measures **velocity component along the beam**; transit-time measures **average velocity over the vessel cross-section** (more accurate for volume-flow estimation). (1)
   2. Transit-time is **independent of the Doppler angle** in the same way — it depends only on geometry, not on $\cos\theta$ in the denominator. (1)
   3. Transit-time works only on **transonic beams** (invasively around the vessel); Doppler can be **non-invasive** through the skin. (1)
   4. Transit-time is **angle-independent in principle** because the up- and down-stream paths share the same geometry; systematic errors largely cancel. (1)
   5. Transit-time is preferred for **absolute volume flow** (mL/s) in cardiac-output measurement; Doppler is preferred for **velocity profile** and non-invasive screening. (1)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Doppler equation** $f_d = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ — factor of 2, units consistent.
- [ ] **Speed of sound in soft tissue** $c = 1540$ m/s.
- [ ] **Typical transducer frequencies** 2 MHz (deep vessels) and 5–10 MHz (superficial).
- [ ] **Piezoelectric effect** — PZT or PVDF crystal acts as both transmitter and receiver.
- [ ] **Couplant gel** is mandatory to bridge the acoustic-impedance mismatch between air and skin.
- [ ] **SBP** = cuff pressure at the **first** Doppler burst; **DBP** = cuff pressure at the **last** burst.
- [ ] **MAP formula** $MAP = DBP + \tfrac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ — derived assuming systolic duty cycle of 1/3.
- [ ] **Transit-time vs Doppler** — transit-time measures volume flow; Doppler measures velocity.
- [ ] **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$; reflection coefficient $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$.
- [ ] **Block diagram** — must include: oscillator, transducer, RF amplifier, demodulator, cuff, pressure sensor, ADC, MCU, display.
- [ ] **Advantages** — non-invasive, noise-immune, usable on neonates/obese, automatable, continuous-monitoring capable.
- [ ] **Limitations** — angle-dependent, motion artifacts, expensive, requires skilled probe placement.
- [ ] **Binomial approximation** $\dfrac{1+x}{1-x} \approx 1 + 2x$ is the key mathematical step linking the exact two-source Doppler formula to the engineering form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plethysmography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT864 - BIOMECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plethysmography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plethysmography** is a non-invasive biomedical measurement technique used to detect and quantify **volumetric changes** within a body part, organ, or the entire body. The term originates from the Greek words *plethysmos* (increase) and *graphein* (to write). The graphical record of these volume fluctuations is called a **plethysmogram**.
