<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Human Body as an Electrical Element

The body is modelled as a *non-linear, frequency-dependent impedance network*. Three dominant current paths exist:

1. **Skin-to-skin** (limb to limb) — most common macro-shock path.
2. **Skin-to-heart** (right hand → left foot) — ventricular fibrillation risk.
3. **Direct heart contact** (intracardiac catheter) — micro-shock.

### 2.1.1 Body Resistance Model

$$R_{\text{body}} = R_{\text{skin}} + R_{\text{internal}} + R_{\text{skin}}$$

where:
- $R_{\text{skin}}$: skin resistance, typically **10 kΩ** (dry) down to **1 kΩ** (wet/broken skin).
- $R_{\text{internal}}$: tissue + blood resistance, approximately **300 Ω** (hand-to-hand) to **100 Ω** (across chest).

> [!NOTE]
> The skin's resistance is *non-linear*: at low voltages (< 10 V) it dominates, but above ~50 V skin breaks down and resistance collapses to the internal value. This is why even a 50 V source can be lethal when skin is wet.

### 2.1.2 Skin Impedance (Frequency-Dependent)

The skin behaves as a parallel RC network:

$$Z_{\text{skin}}(f) = \frac{R_s}{1 + (2\pi f R_s C_s)^2} \cdot \left[ 1 - j(2\pi f R_s C_s) \right]$$

As $f \to \infty$, $Z_{\text{skin}} \to 0$. This is why **high-frequency electrosurgical units (≈ 400 kHz)** can cut tissue despite low voltage, and why **50 Hz mains** is the most dangerous frequency for ventricular fibrillation.

## 2.2 Threshold Currents and Physiological Effects

| Effect | DC (mA) | 50/60 Hz AC (mA rms) |
|---|---|---|
| Threshold of perception | ≈ 2 | ≈ 0.5 – 1 |
| Let-go threshold (men) | ≈ 76 | ≈ 16 |
| Let-go threshold (women) | ≈ 51 | ≈ 10.5 |
| Respiratory paralysis | ≈ 18 | ≈ 18 |
| Ventricular fibrillation (macro, 5 s) | ≈ 500 | ≈ 100 |
| Severe burns | > 1 A | > 1 A |
| Defibrillation threshold (therapeutic) | — | 20 – 360 (deliberate, synchronized) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 50/60 Hz is uniquely dangerous:**
> The myocardium's relative refractory period lasts ~ 200 ms (≈ 10 cycles at 50 Hz). This perfectly overlaps with the *vulnerable window* of the cardiac cycle, so mains-frequency current can easily re-trigger the ventricles into chaotic fibrillation.

## 2.3 Leakage Currents — Definitions (IEC 60601-1)

| Symbol | Name | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $I_{\text{Earth}}$ | Earth Leakage Current | Flows from mains to protective earth through the chassis |
| $I_{\text{Encl}}$ | Enclosure (Touch) Leakage Current | From chassis to external ground via patient/user |
| $I_{\text{Pat}}$ | Patient Leakage Current | From patient connection to earth |
| $I_{\text{PatAux}}$ | Patient Auxiliary Current | Between two patient electrodes, not through earth |
| $I_{\text{SFC}}$ | Single-Fault Current | Leakage under any *single* insulation breakdown |

Limits (Normal Condition, NC):

| Current | Type B | Type BF | Type CF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Patient leakage (NC) | 100 µA | 100 µA | **10 µA** |
| Patient leakage (SFC) | 500 µA | 500 µA | 50 µA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Type CF** (Cardiac Floating) is the *highest safety class* and is mandatory for any device that makes *direct contact with the heart* (e.g., intra-cardiac catheters, pacemaker programmers, intra-aortic balloon pump). This is the standard that protects against **micro-shock**.

## 2.4 Protection Schemes Against Shock

### 2.4.1 Equipment Classes

- **Class I**: Mains-powered with *protective earth* (PE). Single-fault → chassis becomes live.
- **Class II**: *Double-insulated* (no PE); relies on reinforced insulation.
- **Class III**: *Battery / SELV-powered* (Safety Extra-Low Voltage < 25 V AC or 60 V DC).

### 2.4.2 Defibrillation-Proof Isolation

A defibrillator delivers up to **360 J** into a **50 Ω** load — peak voltage ≈ **5 kV**. Every patient input that may be connected to a patient during defibrillation must:

- Withstand a **5 kV** test pulse without damage.
- Limit the energy delivered to the monitor's input to < 1 mJ.
- Use a *neutral electrode* and a *resettable fuse* in series.

### 2.4.3 Isolated Power Systems (IPS) and Line Isolation Monitors (LIM)

In an *Isolated Power System* (often an IT-earthed system, e.g., in operating theatres), the neutral is *not* tied to earth. A single fault does *not* trip a breaker; instead, a **Line Isolation Monitor (LIM)** continuously measures the system's impedance to earth and alarms if it falls below **25 kΩ** (for 230 V mains) — the threshold below which a second fault would become hazardous.

$$I_{\text{fault, max}} = \frac{V_{\text{mains}}}{Z_{\text{isolation}}}$$

For $V_{\text{mains}} = 230$ V and $Z_{\text{isolation}} = 25$ kΩ, $I_{\text{fault, max}} \approx 9.2$ mA — below the let-go threshold.

## 2.5 Centralized Patient Monitoring System — Architecture

A modern CPMS follows a **three-tier hierarchy**:

| Tier | Function | Typical Hardware |
|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 — *Acquisition* | Bedside monitors, sensors, lead cables | Multi-parameter monitor modules |
| Tier 2 — *Communication* | Hard-wired Ethernet (100BASE-TX) or Wi-Fi (5 GHz) | Hospital LAN, HL7 gateway |
| Tier 3 — *Display/Archive* | Central station, server, EMR integration | 2–8 monitor video wall, RAID storage |

### 2.5.1 Signal Conditioning Chain (per parameter)

1. **Electrode/sensor** — Ag/AgCl ECG electrode, SpO₂ photodiode, NIBP cuff.
2. **Amplification** — INA (Instrumentation Amplifier), gain 100 – 1000.
3. **Filtering** — 0.05 Hz high-pass (DC removal) + 40 Hz low-pass (line-noise).
4. **Isolation** — optocoupler / isolation transformer (> 4 kV).
5. **A/D conversion** — 16-bit, ≥ 500 Hz sample rate for ECG.
6. **DSP** — QRS detection (Pan–Tompkins), SpO₂ ratio-of-ratios, artifact rejection.
7. **Display & alarm** — colour-coded limits, priority tags.

### 2.5.2 Data Protocols

- **HL7 (Health Level Seven)** — clinical messaging standard.
- **DICOM** — imaging and waveform interchange.
- **IEEE 11073** — point-of-care medical device communication.
- **TCP/IP / UDP** transport with QoS for real-time waveforms.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these expressions for the KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE).**

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ohm's law for body | $I = V \div R_{\text{body}}$ | A | $R_{\text{body}}$ dominates path |
| Macro-shock fibr. threshold | $I_{\text{VF,m}} \approx 100$ mA | mA | hand-to-hand, 50 Hz, > 1 s |
| Micro-shock fibr. threshold | $I_{\text{VF,μ}} \approx 10$ µA | µA | direct cardiac contact |
| Skin impedance (parallel RC) | $Z_{\text{skin}} = R_s \vert\vert (1 \div j\omega C_s)$ | Ω | freq.-dependent |
| Defibrillator peak voltage | $V_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{(2E \div C)}$ | V | energy $E$, capacitance $C$ |
| Defibrillator stored energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | J | capacitor discharge model |
| Patient energy in defibrillation | $E_{\text{del}} = V^{2} \cdot t \div R$ | J | 50 Ω typical impedance |
| Limiting resistor for patient lead | $R_{\text{lim}} \geq V_{\text{iso}} \div I_{\text{lim,max}}$ | Ω | sets safe current |
| ECG QRS detection threshold | $V_{\text{th}} = 0.5 \, V_{\text{peak,QRS}}$ | mV | Pan–Tompkins |
| SpO₂ ratio of ratios | $R = (AC_{660} \div DC_{660}) \div (AC_{940} \div DC_{940})$ | — | empirical lookup |
| Heart rate from R-R interval | $HR = 60 \div \Delta t_{RR}$ | bpm | $\Delta t_{RR}$ in seconds |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{signal}} \div V_{\text{noise}})$ | dB | ≥ 40 dB desirable |
| Common-mode rejection | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}(A_d \div A_{cm})$ | dB | ≥ 80 dB ECG |
| Mains frequency (India/Kerala) | $f = 50$ Hz | Hz | single-phase 230 V |
| Max earth leakage (Type B, NC) | $I_{\text{earth}} \leq 500$ µA | µA | IEC 60601-1 |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the 1 mA / 10 mA / 100 mA / 10 µA ladder** — these are the four magic numbers that recur in every shock-hazard question. They are the *verbal skeleton* of a 14-mark answer.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating rooms** mandate isolated power systems so that a single fault does not black out the room mid-surgery.
- **MRI rooms** (3 T scanners) demand non-magnetic, non-ferrous monitoring and special filtering for RF interference — extending the shock-hazard analysis to RF burns.
- **Telemedicine / remote ICU** hubs (e.g., Apollo, KIMS in India) are full-blown CPMS, where a single central station monitors 60–120 beds across multiple hospitals.
- **Defibrillator-monitor combinations** (e.g., Philips HeartStart, Mindray BeneHeart) embody all isolation, energy-limiting, and CPMS concepts in one chassis.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Defibrillator Peak Voltage from Stored Energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem statement:** A defibrillator has a capacitance of $C = 32$ µF charged to a stored energy of $E = 360$ J. Compute the peak voltage, the peak current through a 50 Ω patient, and the time constant of discharge.

### Step 1 — Energy stored in a capacitor

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}$$

Solve for $V$:

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{2E}{C}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{peak}} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 360}{32 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{720}{32 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \sqrt{22.5 \times 10^{6}} \\
&\approx 4743 \text{ V} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This ≈ 4.7 kV matches the IEC-required test level of 5 kV. The 360 J biphasic waveform used in modern defibrillators keeps the peak voltage lower than monophasic designs.

### Step 3 — Peak current

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{peak}} &= \frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{R_{\text{patient}}} \\
&= \frac{4743}{50} \\
&\approx 94.9 \text{ A} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — RC discharge time constant

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= R \cdot C \\
&= 50 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 1.6 \text{ ms} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The biphasic pulse is truncated at ~ 4 ms, after which ≈ 95 % of the energy has been delivered.

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam pitfall:** Students forget to use *rms* vs *peak* correctly. A 230 V AC mains has a peak of $230 \cdot \sqrt{2} \approx 325$ V. Defibrillator peak is on the order of *kilovolts* — a thousand times larger.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Body Current and Let-Go Threshold

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem statement:** A clinician accidentally contacts a 230 V AC live conductor with dry hands (assume $R_{\text{body}} = 2$ kΩ hand-to-foot path). Compute the body current and determine the physiological outcome.

### Step 1 — Apply Ohm's Law

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{body}} &= \frac{V_{\text{mains}}}{R_{\text{body}}} \\
&= \frac{230}{2 \times 10^{3}} \\
&= 0.115 \text{ A} \\
&= 115 \text{ mA} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compare to threshold table

- 115 mA > 100 mA → **ventricular fibrillation risk** (macro-shock).
- 115 mA > 16 mA → **let-go exceeded** (muscle tetany; cannot release).

> [!NOTE]
> With *wet* hands, $R_{\text{body}} \to 1$ kΩ, so the current becomes **230 mA** — a near-certain VF within seconds. This is why ICU staff are trained to *never touch a patient and a conductive surface simultaneously*.

### Step 3 — Power dissipated

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= I^{2} R \\
&= (0.115)^{2} \times 2000 \\
&\approx 26.5 \text{ W} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A small fraction of this is deposited as heat in tissue; burns appear at the entry/exit points for currents > 1 A or durations > 10 s.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Micro-shock via Intracardiac Catheter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem statement:** A patient has a central venous catheter. A leakage of 8 µA reaches the heart. Compare to the micro-shock fibrillation threshold (10 µA).

### Step 1 — Express current in µA

$I = 8$ µA

### Step 2 — Compare to threshold

$$
\frac{I}{I_{\text{VF,μ}}} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8
$$

The leakage is **80 % of the fibrillation threshold** — non-fibrillating *but* borderline. A simultaneous 2 µA increase (e.g., from another lead) would tip the heart into VF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design implication:** All equipment that contacts the heart must keep *patient leakage current* ≤ **10 µA** under normal conditions and ≤ **50 µA** under single fault. This is the *Type CF* requirement.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Line Isolation Monitor Trip Level

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem statement:** An OR uses an IT-isolated 230 V, 50 Hz system. The LIM is set to alarm at $Z_{\text{isolation}} = 25$ kΩ. Calculate the maximum fault current.

### Step 1 — Apply Ohm's law

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{fault, max}} &= \frac{V_{\text{mains}}}{Z_{\text{isolation}}} \\
&= \frac{230}{25 \times 10^{3}} \\
&= 9.2 \text{ mA} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compare to let-go threshold

$I_{\text{fault, max}} = 9.2$ mA < 16 mA (let-go for men) → patient can release.

> [!NOTE]
> If $Z_{\text{isolation}}$ were allowed to drop to 5 kΩ, the fault current would rise to **46 mA** — exceeding the let-go threshold and creating a macro-shock hazard. This is exactly what the LIM prevents.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Patient Auxiliary Current Limit (Type CF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem statement:** A monitor has two patient leads with 50 kΩ input impedance each. Calculate the auxiliary current that flows between them if 1 mV appears across the input stage.

### Step 1 — Apply Ohm's law to the loop

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{aux}} &= \frac{V_{\text{noise}}}{2 R_{\text{in}}} \\
&= \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 50 \times 10^{3}} \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-8} \text{ A} \\
&= 10 \text{ nA} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compare to threshold

10 nA ≪ 10 µA → *well within Type CF limit*. Good design.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Shock Hazards vs Defensive Design

| Hazard Source | Magnitude | Defensive Design | Standard / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct mains contact | 230 V, 50 Hz | Double insulation, PE | Class I / II |
| Leakage via chassis | 100 µA | Reinforced insulation, EMI filter | IEC 60601-1, Type B |
| Leakage via patient lead | 100 µA | Optocoupler isolation, 4 kV barrier | Type BF |
| Direct cardiac contact | 10 µA | Isolated patient module, floating inputs | Type CF |
| Defibrillator pulse | 4.7 kV peak | Neutral electrode, gas-discharge tube, current-limiter | IEC 60601-1, 5 kV test |
| Electrosurgical interference | 400 kHz, 300 W | Notch filter at 50/60 Hz, shielding | CISPR 11 |
| Static discharge (ESD) | 8 kV air | Conductive floor, wrist straps, anti-static | IEC 61000-4-2 |
| MRI RF burns | 64 MHz, 3 T | Special non-ferrous leads, SAR limit | IEC 60601-2-33 |

> [!TIP]
> The matrix above is a *ready-made 7-mark table* for a KTU 14-mark question. Reproduce it from memory with one example per row to score full marks.

---

## 3.7 CPMS — Latency & Bandwidth Calculation (Optional)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem statement:** A 12-lead ECG sampled at 500 Hz, 16-bit resolution, is transmitted from 32 bedside monitors to a central station over a 100 Mbps Ethernet LAN. Calculate the total bandwidth requirement.

### Step 1 — Per-channel data rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{ch}} &= f_s \times n \times \text{bits per sample} \\
&= 500 \times 12 \times 16 \\
&= 96\,000 \text{ bps per lead} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

With 8 leads simultaneously acquired (standard 5-lead configuration plus derived leads), per monitor:

$$
R_{\text{monitor}} = 96\,000 \times 8 = 768 \text{ kbps}
$$

### Step 2 — Aggregate 32 monitors

$$
R_{\text{total}} = 768 \text{ kbps} \times 32 = 24.6 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 100 Mbps link has ~75 % usable throughput (~ 75 Mbps), so it can carry ~100 such monitors — typical for a hospital network. Waveform latency remains < 100 ms end-to-end, satisfying the IEC 80001-1 alarm-delivery requirement of < 2 s.

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are designed for *block-level functional architecture*, following the engine's anti-keyword and label-formatting safeguards. Every node ID is alphanumeric, prefixed with a letter, and contains clean uppercase text only.

## 4.1 Macro-Shock vs Micro-Shock Current Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[MAINS 230 V 50 HZ] --> B1[FAULTY CHASSIS]
    B1 --> C1[USER SKIN DRY R 2 K OHM]
    C1 --> D1[BODY INTERNAL R 300 OHM]
    D1 --> E1[EARTH RETURN]
    E1 --> F1[MACRO SHOCK I 115 MA]

    A2[MONITOR MAINS] --> B2[LEAKAGE FROM CAPACITOR]
    B2 --> C2[PATIENT LEAD TO CATHETER]
    C2 --> D2[HEART DIRECT CONTACT]
    D2 --> E2[RETURN VIA FLUID COLUMN]
    E2 --> F2[MICRO SHOCK I 10 UA]
```

**Interpretation:**
- *Upper path* (macro-shock) shows current from mains, through a chassis fault, into the user's body, and back to earth. Typical current 115 mA — fatal.
- *Lower path* (micro-shock) shows a 10 µA leakage entering the heart via a catheter. Even this is sufficient to fibrillate.

## 4.2 CPMS Three-Tier Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1A[BEDSIDE MONITOR 1 ECG SPO2 NIBP] --> NET[HOSPITAL LAN 100 MBPS]
    T1B[BEDSIDE MONITOR 2 ECG SPO2 NIBP] --> NET
    T1C[BEDSIDE MONITOR N ECG SPO2 NIBP] --> NET
    NET --> HUB[CENTRAL STATION DISPLAY]
    HUB --> SRV[HIS AND EMR SERVER]
    SRV --> ARC[RAID DATA ARCHIVE]
    HUB --> ALM[ALARM MANAGEMENT CONSOLE]
    ALM --> NUR[CLINICAL TEAM]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Tier 1 (bedside monitors) acquires signals.
- Tier 2 (LAN) transports data.
- Tier 3 (central station, server, archive) provides display, alarm, and storage.

## 4.3 Patient Isolation Barrier Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EL[ECG ELECTRODE] --> AMP[INA INSTRUMENTATION AMP GAIN 1000]
    AMP --> FILT[0.05 TO 40 HZ BANDPASS]
    FILT --> ISO[OPTOCOUPLER ISOLATION 4 KV]
    ISO --> ADC[16 BIT ADC 500 HZ]
    ADC --> DSP[PAN TOMPKINS QRS DETECTION]
    DSP --> DSP2[SPO2 RATIO OF RATIOS]
    DSP2 --> OUT[NETWORK TRANSMITTER TCP IP]
    MAINS[MAINS 230 V] --> PWR[ISOLATED DC DC CONVERTER 5 KV]
    PWR --> AMP
    PWR --> FILT
    PWR --> ISO
```

**Interpretation:**
- The patient side (left) is *galvanically isolated* from the mains side (right) by a 4 kV optocoupler barrier.
- Power is also routed through an isolated DC-DC converter, ensuring no direct electrical path exists between the patient and the mains.

## 4.4 Defibrillation-Proof Input Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PAD[DEFIBRILLATOR PAD] --> RES1[CURRENT LIMITING RESISTOR 10 K OHM]
    RES1 --> NEUT[NEUTRAL ELECTRODE NETWORK]
    NEUT --> GDT[GAS DISCHARGE TUBE 5 KV]
    GDT --> ESD[TVS DIODE 6.8 V]
    ESD --> AMP[FRONT END AMPLIFIER]
    AMP --> OUT[MICROCONTROLLER]
    FUSE[RESETTABLE FUSE] --> RES1
```

**Interpretation:**
- A 5 kV defibrillator pulse is shunted by the gas-discharge tube before reaching the sensitive front-end amplifier.
- The current-limiting resistor and resettable fuse protect the patient-side input from over-current.

## 4.5 LIM and IT-Earthed Power System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[MAINS 230 V] --> ISO[ISOLATION TRANSFORMER 1 TO 1]
    ISO --> BUS[IT EARTH BUS NO NEUTRAL EARTH LINK]
    BUS --> L1[OT LIGHT 1]
    BUS --> L2[MONITOR 1]
    BUS --> L3[DEFIBRILLATOR 1]
    LIM[LIM LINE ISOLATION MONITOR] --> BUS
    LIM --> ALR[ALARM WHEN Z LESS THAN 25 K OHM]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The isolation transformer floats the OR from earth.
- The LIM continuously measures impedance to earth and alarms before a second fault becomes hazardous.

## 4.6 End-to-End CPMS Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[PATIENT A ECG] --> B1[BEDSIDE MONITOR A]
    P2[PATIENT B ECG] --> B2[BEDSIDE MONITOR B]
    B1 --> SW[NETWORK SWITCH GIGABIT]
    B2 --> SW
    SW --> CS[CENTRAL STATION]
    SW --> SRV[HL7 GATEWAY]
    SRV --> HIS[HOSPITAL INFORMATION SYSTEM]
    CS --> NURSE[NURSE WORKSTATION]
    HIS --> DR[PHYSICIAN EMR PORTAL]
    CS --> ALM[ALARM CONSOLE]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The HL7 gateway is the *interoperability layer* between the bedside stream and the EMR.
- Alarms flow independently to the alarm console for low-latency response.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These model the KTU University Exam Part A pattern. Each answer should be ~ 1 page of concise writing (≈ 150 words).

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Define *micro-shock*. How does it differ from macro-shock in terms of current threshold and clinical significance?

**Model Answer:**

*Micro-shock* is the passage of electric current through the heart *directly*, typically via an intracardiac catheter, pacemaker lead, or conductive saline column. Because the current bypasses the skin's resistance, the *fibrillation threshold* drops by *four orders of magnitude* — from ≈ 100 mA in macro-shock to as low as **10 µA** in micro-shock. Clinical significance: any equipment that may contact the heart (e.g., central-line monitors, pacemaker programmers, intra-aortic balloon pumps) must therefore meet *Type CF* isolation standards under IEC 60601-1, with patient leakage ≤ 10 µA under normal conditions. The presence of multiple patient-connected devices, as in a CPMS, amplifies this risk and demands per-bedside isolation, not just system-level isolation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — What is a *Centralized Patient Monitoring System*? List its four primary functions.

**Model Answer:**

A *Centralized Patient Monitoring System (CPMS)* is a networked clinical architecture in which physiological signals (ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, respiration, temperature) from multiple bedside patients are acquired, transmitted over a hospital LAN, displayed on a central station, and archived on a server. Its four primary functions are:

1. *Continuous multi-parameter surveillance* of every connected patient with sub-second waveform latency.
2. *Centralized alarm management*, including intelligent prioritization to reduce alarm fatigue.
3. *Data archival and trend analysis* for clinical decision support and legal record.
4. *Interoperability* with Hospital Information Systems (HIS) and Electronic Medical Records (EMR) using HL7/DICOM protocols.

A CPMS must comply with IEC 60601-1 patient-safety standards at every bedside node.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module 4 Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently (a) confuse *patient leakage* with *earth leakage*, (b) forget the 4-order-of-magnitude gap between macro and micro-shock thresholds, (c) omit units in formulas, and (d) skip the *single-fault condition* in IEC 60601-1. Each omission costs ~ 1–2 marks. Always state *Normal Condition (NC)* vs *Single-Fault Condition (SFC)* explicitly.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **A(a)** [7 marks] Explain the various mechanisms by which electrical shock is delivered to a patient in a hospital environment. Compare macro-shock and micro-shock with reference to threshold currents, current paths, and relevant IEC 60601-1 classifications.
>
> **A(b)** [7 marks] With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the *patient isolation barrier* used in a bedside monitor. Compute the limiting resistor value needed to keep the patient leakage current below 10 µA when the isolation barrier breaks down to 230 V mains.

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**Mechanisms of shock delivery in a hospital:**

1. **Direct contact with a live chassis** (macro-shock): a Class I device with a broken earth allows the chassis to float at mains potential. The user touches it, current flows hand-to-foot.

2. **Leakage current through capacitive coupling**: even well-insulated equipment has parasitic capacitance (~ 2 – 5 nF) between live parts and the chassis. At 50 Hz this gives:
$$
I_{\text{leak}} = V_{\text{mains}} \cdot 2 \pi f C \approx 230 \cdot 314 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.22 \text{ mA}
$$
This is sub-let-go but exceeds the patient leakage limit of 100 µA. Hence the need for EMI-filter capacitors and Y-capacitor ratings.

3. **Patient-applied part (Type BF/CF)** faults: if the input protection of a monitor fails, mains voltage can appear on a lead connected to the patient.

4. **Leakage from an external source via a catheter** (micro-shock): a saline-filled catheter acts as a low-resistance conductor. A few microamperes from any connected device can reach the heart.

**Macro vs Micro comparison:**

| Parameter | Macro-shock | Micro-shock |
|---|---|---|
| Current path | Skin-to-skin / skin-to-heart | Direct heart contact |
| Resistance | 1 – 10 kΩ (skin) | 100 – 300 Ω (tissue) |
| VF threshold | 100 mA | 10 µA |
| IEC class | B / BF | CF (cardiac floating) |
| Patient leakage limit | 100 µA (NC) | 10 µA (NC) |
| Equipment example | Pulse oximeter | Pacemaker programmer |

> This is the standard 4-marks-per-element structure; total 7 marks with the comparison table.

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**Block diagram of patient isolation barrier:**

(Pause and reproduce the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.3 with paper-and-pen clarity: *Electrode → INA → BPF → Optocoupler (4 kV) → ADC → DSP → Network*; *Mains → Isolated DC-DC → Patient-side*.)

**Resistor calculation:**

The worst-case voltage across the broken isolation barrier is the mains peak (325 V). To keep patient leakage below 10 µA:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{lim}} &= \frac{V_{\text{mains,peak}}}{I_{\text{lim,max}}} \\
&= \frac{230 \cdot \sqrt{2}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{325}{10^{-5}} \\
&= 32.5 \text{ MΩ} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

A 33 MΩ resistor in series with the patient lead achieves this. In practice, the isolation *opto-coupler* provides several GΩ; the resistor is a backup.

**Design verification:** at 230 V across 33 MΩ, the current is:

$$
I = 230 / 33 \times 10^{6} = 6.97 \text{ µA} < 10 \text{ µA} \;\;\; ✓
$$

This is *below* the Type CF limit, satisfying IEC 60601-1.

(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for derivation, 2 for verification.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

> **B(a)** [7 marks] Describe the architecture of a *Centralized Patient Monitoring System (CPMS)*. With a block diagram, explain the three-tier hierarchy and the role of HL7 in interoperability.
>
> **B(b)** [7 marks] A defibrillator uses a 32 µF capacitor charged to deliver 360 J. Compute the peak voltage, peak current into a 50 Ω patient, and the discharge time constant. State two safety features in a CPMS that allow continued monitoring *during* defibrillation.

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**Three-tier architecture of CPMS:**

| Tier | Function | Hardware |
|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 — Acquisition | Bedside multi-parameter monitors with patient cables | ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, IBP, EtCO₂ modules |
| Tier 2 — Communication | Hard-wired LAN (100 Mbps Ethernet) or Wi-Fi (5 GHz) | Switches, HL7 gateway, routers |
| Tier 3 — Display/Archive | Central station, server, EMR integration | Video wall, RAID server, HIS |

**Block diagram:** (reproduce the Section 4.2 flowchart on paper).

**Role of HL7:**
HL7 (Health Level Seven) is a messaging standard that lets the CPMS *export* structured data (vital signs, alarms, waveform snippets) into the hospital's HIS/EMR. Each bedside monitor generates HL7 v2.x messages (e.g., ORU^R01 for observations) which the HL7 gateway routes to the central station and the EMR server. This achieves *interoperability* — physicians can review trends from any workstation, and billing/audit data is automatically captured.

> Mention also **IEEE 11073** (point-of-care device communication) and **DICOM** (waveform interchange) as supporting standards for full marks (1 extra mark).

(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for tier explanation, 2 for HL7, 1 for additional standards.)

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**Step 1 — Peak voltage:**

$$
V_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{\frac{2E}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 360}{32 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{22.5 \times 10^{6}} \approx 4743 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Peak current into 50 Ω patient:**

$$
I_{\text{peak}} = \frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{R} = \frac{4743}{50} \approx 94.9 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Discharge time constant:**

$$
\tau = R C = 50 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \text{ ms}
$$

The biphasic pulse truncates at ~ 4 ms, delivering > 95 % of the stored energy.

**Two safety features that allow continued monitoring during defibrillation:**

1. **Neutral-electrode network and gas-discharge tube (GDT) at the input** that shunts the 5 kV pulse to chassis ground, preventing damage to the front-end amplifier and the patient simultaneously.
2. **Current-limiting resistors in series with patient leads (≥ 10 kΩ)** and **resettable fuses** (PTC) that absorb the energy and recover automatically after the shock.
3. (Bonus) **Optocoupler isolation (4 kV RMS)** that decouples the patient side from the rest of the monitor, ensuring the *display unit* and network card survive.

(7 marks: 2 for voltage, 1 for current, 1 for τ, 3 for safety features.)

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (final-minute glance before entering the exam hall):**

### A. Electrical Shock Hazards
- *Threshold of perception* ≈ **1 mA** (50 Hz skin contact).
- *Let-go threshold* ≈ **10 mA** (muscle tetany, victim cannot release).
- *Ventricular fibrillation (macro-shock)* ≈ **100 mA** for > 1 s.
- *Ventricular fibrillation (micro-shock)* ≈ **10 µA** direct heart contact.
- *Frequency 50/60 Hz* is uniquely dangerous because it overlaps the cardiac refractory period.
- *Body resistance* = $R_{\text{skin,1}} + R_{\text{internal}} + R_{\text{skin,2}}$; collapses with wet or broken skin.
- *Skin impedance* is *non-linear and frequency-dependent*; falls to internal value above ~ 50 V.
- *Macro-shock* → external contact, ~ 100 mA threshold, mitigated by Class I/II insulation, PE, double insulation.
- *Micro-shock* → direct cardiac contact, ~ 10 µA threshold, mitigated by Type CF isolation, optocouplers, isolated DC-DC converters.
- *IEC 60601-1* governs leakage limits; *Type B, BF, CF* are the three patient-protection classes.
- *Earth leakage (Class I, NC)* ≤ 500 µA; *Type CF patient leakage (NC)* ≤ 10 µA.
- *Defibrillator-proof* inputs must withstand **5 kV** test and limit patient energy to < 1 mJ.
- *ESU interference* suppressed by 50/60 Hz notch filters; ESU operates at ~ 400 kHz, *not* lethal but disrupts monitoring.
- *LIM* trips at isolation impedance < 25 kΩ in IT-earthed OR systems.
- *IPS (Isolated Power System)* uses an isolation transformer with no neutral-to-earth link.

### B. Centralized Patient Monitoring System
- *Three-tier hierarchy*: Acquisition (bedside) → Communication (LAN) → Display/Archive (central station, server, EMR).
- *Bedside monitor functions*: signal acquisition, amplification, filtering, A/D, DSP, display, alarm.
- *Central station* shows all patients simultaneously, manages alarms, allows trend review.
- *Communication standards*: HL7 (clinical messaging), DICOM (imaging/waveform), IEEE 11073 (point-of-care).
- *Network*: 100 Mbps Ethernet is standard; latency < 100 ms end-to-end.
- *Defibrillation-monitor integration* uses neutral electrode + GDT + optocoupler for survival.
- *Data archive*: RAID storage, typically 5–10 TB for a 100-bed hospital at 500 Hz ECG × 16 bits × 8 leads.
- *Alarm management* uses three-tier priority (red/yellow/cyan) and 2-second delivery requirement (IEC 80001-1).
- *Cybersecurity* increasingly part of CPMS design (FDA 2023 guidance).

### C. Magic Numbers to Memorize

| Number | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 230 V | Indian mains RMS |
| 50 Hz | Indian mains frequency |
| 1 mA | Perception threshold |
| 10 mA | Let-go threshold |
| 100 mA | Macro-shock VF |
| 10 µA | Micro-shock VF |
| 360 J | Adult defibrillation energy |
| 5 kV | Defibrillation-proof test |
| 4 kV | Patient isolation barrier |
| 25 kΩ | LIM trip impedance |
| 10 nA | 1 mV across 100 kΩ |
| 500 µA | Earth leakage NC limit |
| 100 µA | Patient leakage BF limit |
| 10 µA | Patient leakage CF limit |
| 500 Hz | ECG sample rate |
| 16 bits | ECG A/D resolution |
| 80 dB | ECG CMRR minimum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Advice:** Always end a 14-mark answer with a *summary sentence* linking the answer back to patient safety ("…thus ensuring compliance with IEC 60601-1 and protection against micro-shock fibrillation"). Examiners reward the *systems thinking* mindset, not just formula recitation.
